title: Sakshi Chaitanya
description: The Witness Consciousness
text: the core dialogue where the sage Ashtavakra answers King Janaka's opening inquiries, establishing the pure non-dual (Advaita Vedanta) philosophy of absolute, ever-present freedom.

verses.sanskrit:
कथं ज्ञानमवाप्नोति कथं मुक्तिर्भविष्यति ।
वैराग्यं च कथं प्राप्तमेतद्ब्रूहि मम प्रभो ॥
verses.transliteration:
kathaṃ jñānamavāpnoti kathaṃ muktirbhaviṣyati |
vairāgyaṃ ca kathaṃ prāptametadbrūhi mama prabho ||
verses.english: Janaka said: How is knowledge attained? How is liberation achieved? And how is dispassion reached? Tell me this, my Lord.
verses.commentary: King Janaka asks three foundational spiritual questions that shift consciousness from external identification to the inner witness.

understand.common_misconception: Most people think liberation is a physical place to reach or a state to be manufactured through intense effort after death.
understand.attainment_meaning: In reality, it is the simple cessation of identifying with the mind-body complex, realizing you are already free.

verses.philosophy: This verse sets up the radical non-dual (Advaita) approach: the seeker must first question the very nature of bondage to understand that freedom is not an acquisition, but your fundamental nature.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
मुक्तिं चेच्छसि तात विषयं विषवत्त्यज ।
क्षमार्जवदयातोषसत्यं पीयूषवद्भज ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
muktiṃ cechasi tāta viṣayaṃ viṣavattyaja |
kṣamārjavadayātoṣasatyaṃ pīyūṣavadbhaja ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: My dear child, if you desire liberation, shun the objects of the senses as poison. Seek instead forgiveness, sincerity, kindness, contentment, and truth as nectar.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra begins his instruction by explicitly defining what blocks and what purifies our field of awareness. Sensation-chasing binds energy, whereas moral virtues dissolve ego defense mechanisms.

understand.common_misconception: People think 'shunning sense objects' means forcing physical starvation or running away to a literal cave.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means breaking your psychological dependence on external events for emotional fulfillment, replacing it with internal clarity.

verses.philosophy: Before deep spiritual insight can self-reveal, the turbulent mind must be stabilized. Mental clarity (*Sattva*) serves as the runway for non-dual integration.

verses.sanskrit:
न पृथ्वी न जलं नाग्निर्न वायुर्द्यौर्न वा भवान् ।
एषां साक्षिणमात्मानं चिद्रूपं विद्धि मुक्तये ॥
verses.transliteration:
na pṛthvī na jalaṃ nāgnirna vāyurdyaurna vā bhavān |
eṣāṃ sākṣiṇamātmānaṃ cidrūpaṃ viddhi muktaye ||
verses.english: You are neither earth, water, fire, air, nor space. For liberation, know yourself as the witness of all these, whose essential nature is pure consciousness.
verses.commentary: This is the classic Neti-Neti ('not this, not that') process. Ashtavakra strips away identification with the gross material elements and the physical container.

understand.common_misconception: People often conclude that if they aren't the physical body, they must be some phantom-like ghost trapped inside it.
understand.attainment_meaning: You are the conscious space in which the perception of the body and worldly elements arises and passes.

verses.philosophy: The self (*Atman*) is fundamentally distinct from material phenomena (*Prakriti*). It doesn't reside inside the world; the world appears within it.

verses.sanskrit:
यदि देहं पृथक् कृत्य चिति विश्राम्य तिष्ठसि ।
अधुनैव सुखी शान्तः बन्धमुक्तो भविष्यसि ॥
verses.transliteration:
yadi dehaṃ pṛthak kṛtya citi viśrāmya tiṣṭhasi |
adhunaiva sukhī śāntaḥ bandhamukto bhaviṣyasi ||
verses.english: If you detach yourself from the body and rest in pure consciousness, you will at once become happy, peaceful, and free from all bondage.
verses.commentary: A foundational promise of the Ashtavakra Gita: freedom is instantaneous (*adhunaiva*), requiring zero linear time if the paradigm shift is complete.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that total peace requires decades of sitting in specific postures or mastering breath control systems.
understand.attainment_meaning: The exact millisecond you stop tracking yourself as a biological mechanism, natural freedom is realized.

verses.philosophy: Bondage is an operational illusion caused by continuous mental misidentification. Therefore, freedom cannot be earned; it can only be remembered.

verses.sanskrit:
न त्वं विप्रादिको वर्णः नाश्रमी नाक्षगोचरः ।
असङ्गोऽसि निराकारो विश्वसाक्षी सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
na tvaṃ viprādiko varṇaḥ nāśramī nākṣagocaraḥ |
asaṅgo'si nirākāro viśvasākṣī sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: You do not belong to the Brahmin or any other caste, nor are you at any stage of life. You are unperceivable by the senses. Unattached, formless, and the witness of the universe, be happy.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra demolishes societal tags, biological age, institutional brackets, and conventional identities.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that spiritual growth is dependent on your social role, career path, or religious ranking status.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing your core self has no age, gender, social status, or worldly tracking parameters.

verses.philosophy: The Absolute is completely unattached (*Asanga*) and formless (*Nirakara*). Social structures organize bodies, not infinite awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
धर्माधर्मौ सुखं दुःखं मानसानि न ते विभो ।
न कर्तासि न भोक्तासि मुक्त एवासि सर्वदा ॥
verses.transliteration:
dharmādharmau sukhaṃ duḥkhaṃ mānasāni na te vibho |
na kartāsi na bhoktāsi mukta evāsi sarvadā ||
verses.english: Righteousness and unrighteousness, pleasure and pain, belong only to the mind and not to you, O All-Pervading One. You are neither the doer nor the enjoyer. You are ever free.
verses.commentary: Dualities like moral credits or behavioral guilt reside entirely inside the functional ego computer—not within the supreme witness.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that inner awareness alternates between being happy and sad depending on outer events.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that your mind experiences ups and downs, while *you* are the space observing those shifting states.

verses.philosophy: The Soul is *Akarta* (non-doer) and *Abhokta* (non-experiencer). Karma tracks modifications of ego; it cannot stain pure awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
एको द्रष्टासि सर्वस्य मुक्तप्रायोऽसि सर्वदा ।
अयमेव हि ते बन्धो द्रष्टारं पश्यसीतरम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
eko draṣṭāsi sarvasya muktaprāyo'si sarvadā |
ayameva hi te bandho draṣṭāraṃ paśyasītaram ||
verses.english: You are the solitary witness of everything, and you are always completely free. Your only true bondage is that you perceive the witness as something separate from yourself.
verses.commentary: This hits the center of non-dual practice: treating awareness as an object you have to track down or hunt is the ultimate root of confusion.

understand.common_misconception: Treating consciousness as a special energy object that you can observe or look at outside yourself.
understand.attainment_meaning: You cannot look *at* awareness because you *are* the awareness doing the looking.

verses.philosophy: The ultimate error is the split between subject and object. There is no second entity observing reality; there is only reality viewing itself.

verses.sanskrit:
अहं कर्तेत्यहंमानमहाकृष्णाहिदंशितः ।
नाहं कर्तेति विश्वासामृतं पीत्वा सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃ kartetyahaṃmānamahākṛṣṇāhidaṃśitaḥ |
nāhaṃ karteti viśvāsāmṛtaṃ pītvā sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: You have been bitten by the great black serpent of the ego, which makes you believe 'I am the doer.' Drink the nectar of faith that 'I am not the doer,' and be happy.
verses.commentary: Using sharp imagery, the ego's claim over actions is likened to neurotoxic venom that paralyzes the recognition of true reality.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that 'not being the doer' means abandoning responsibility and sitting completely idle.
understand.attainment_meaning: Letting actions happen naturally through the body without the ego claiming ownership or obsessing over the results.

verses.philosophy: The illusion of independent personal agency generates psychological karma. Dissolving this claim returns the self to its natural state.

verses.sanskrit:
एको विशुद्धबोधोऽहमिति निश्चयवह्निना ।
प्रज्वाल्याज्ञानगहनं वीतशोकः सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
eko viśuddhabodho'hamiti niścayavahnina |
prajvālyājñānagahanaṃ vītaśokaḥ sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: Burn down the dense forest of ignorance with the fire of absolute conviction: 'I am the single, pure consciousness.' Free from distress, be happy.
verses.commentary: Intellectual guessing won't suffice; Ashtavakra calls for a definitive, complete conviction (*niścaya*) to fully burn through mental identification loops.

understand.common_misconception: Hoping that spiritual realization will arrived as a soft, magical feeling or an emotional experience.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is a clear, unshakeable insight that cuts right through internal doubt and illusion.

verses.philosophy: Ignorance (*Avidya*) is not a physical substance; it is an analytical error. Therefore, it is dissolved only by real insight, not by physical actions.

verses.sanskrit:
यत्र विश्वमिदं भाति कल्पितं रज्जुसर्पवत् ।
आनन्दपरमानन्दः स बोधस्त्वं सुखं चर ॥
verses.transliteration:
yatra viśvamidaṃ bhāti kalpitaṃ rajjusarpavat |
ānandaparamānandaḥ sa bodhastvaṃ sukhaṃ cara ||
verses.english: The supreme bliss in which this universe appears imagined, like a snake in a rope—that pure consciousness you are. Live happily.
verses.commentary: Introducing the classic Advaita metaphor: a rope mistaken for a snake causes real fear, but realizing it's just a rope instantly ends the panic.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking you need to physically destroy the world or make your surroundings vanish to find peace.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing the world is an expression of consciousness, which naturally removes its power to bind or terrify you.

verses.philosophy: The world possesses dependent reality (*Vyavaharika*). It relies entirely on pure awareness, which serves as its true substrate.

verses.sanskrit:
मुक्ताभिमानी मुक्तो हि बद्धो बद्धाभिमान्यपि ।
किंवदन्तीह सत्यायै या मतिः सा गतिर्भवेत् ॥
verses.transliteration: muktābhimānī mukto hi baddho baddhābhimānyapi | kiṃvadantīha satyāyai yā matiḥ sā gatirbhavet ||
verses.english: One who considers themselves free is indeed free, and one who considers themselves bound remains bound. As one thinks, so one becomes—this old saying is completely true.
verses.commentary: Your dominant internal orientation dictates your experience of reality. Insisting on tracking yourself as broken fixes you in a state of struggle.

understand.common_misconception: Confusing this insight with modern 'positive thinking' or simply manifesting personal desires.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is realizing that identifying with structural bondage keeps the illusion of limitation alive.

verses.philosophy: Consciousness shapes its own contextual limitations through focus. Reclaiming your true identity breaks the cycle of perceived restriction.

verses.sanskrit:
आत्मा साक्षी विभुः पूर्णः एको मुक्तश्चिदाशयः ।
अक्रियो निःसङ्गः स्पृहाशान्तो भ्रमवाद्विव ॥
verses.transliteration:
ātmā sākṣī vibhuḥ pūrṇaḥ eko muktaści dāśayaḥ |
akriyo niḥsaṅgaḥ spṛhāśānto bhramādvia iva ||
verses.english: The true Self is the all-pervading, perfect, solitary witness. It is free, conscious, actionless, unattached, desireless, and quiet. Through illusion alone does it seem involved in the world.
verses.commentary: A complete summary of the attributes of pure identity. The Self isn't evolving toward perfection; it is already the unchanging foundation of experience.

understand.common_misconception: Viewing the Self as something incomplete that needs to collect experiences to grow.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing your core identity is already whole, observing life without needing things to complete it.

verses.philosophy: The nature of the absolute is inherently complete (*Purna*). It does not undergo transitions, modifications, or spiritual progress.

verses.sanskrit:
कूटस्थं बोधमद्वैतमात्मानं परिभावय ।
आभासोऽहं भ्रमं मुक्त्वा भावं बाह्यमथान्तरम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
kūṭasthaṃ bodhamadvaitamātmānaṃ paribhāvaya |
ābhāso'haṃ bhramaṃ muktvā bhāvaṃ bāhyamathāntaram ||
verses.english: Meditate on the unchanging, non-dual Self. Drop the illusion of being an isolated reflection, and leave behind both internal and external attachments.
verses.commentary: The term *Kutastha* refers to an anvil, which remains completely unmoved despite being struck by countless hammers. The Self is that solid foundation.

understand.common_misconception: Believing your spiritual journey is about repairing your human ego or personality structure.
understand.attainment_meaning: Stepping back from the ego entirely, realizing it is just a passing reflection on the surface of your awareness.

verses.philosophy: The individual personality (*Jiva*) is a localized reflection of pure awareness. Freedom means shifting your identity from the reflection to the source.

verses.sanskrit:
देहाभिमानपाशेन बद्धोऽसि तात सर्वदा ।
बोधोऽहं ज्ञानखङ्गेन तन्निकृत्य सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
dehābhimānapāśena baddho'si tāta sarvada |
bodho'haṃ jñānakhaṅgena tannikṛtya sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: My child, you have long been bound by the rope of body-identification. Cut through that bond with the sword of knowledge—'I am pure awareness'—and be happy.
verses.commentary: Body-identification functions like a tight cord around your awareness. Ashtavakra points to sharp discrimination (*Jnana*) to cut it clean away.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that untying this bond requires slow, painful effort or physical discipline.
understand.attainment_meaning: Using clear insight to see through the illusion immediately, which cuts the attachment without an uphill struggle.

verses.philosophy: The root cause of human suffering (*Samsara*) is *Deha-Abhimana* (body-ownership). Shifting identity to awareness brings immediate release.

verses.sanskrit:
निःसङ्गो निष्क्रियोऽसि त्वं स्वप्रकाशो निरञ्जनः ।
अयमेव हि ते बन्धो समाधिमनुतिष्ठसि ॥
verses.transliteration:
niḥsaṅgo niṣkriyo'si tvaṃ svaprakāśo nirañjanaḥ |
ayameva hi te bandho samādhimanutiṣṭhasi ||
verses.english: You are unattached, actionless, self-luminous, and stainless. Your actual bondage is that you keep practicing formal meditation to capture this.
verses.commentary: One of the most radical statements in the text: Ashtavakra points out that forcing your mind into deep states of concentration actually reinforces the false idea that you aren't already free.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that continuous spiritual exercises and trance states are the ultimate goal of freedom.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that trying to quiet the mind implies you think you are the mind, when you are actually the awareness observing it.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Sva-Prakasha* (self-luminous) and requires no mental techniques to shine. Relying on states of meditation makes your freedom dependent on conditions.

verses.sanskrit:
त्वया व्याप्तमिदं विश्वं त्वयि प्रोतं यथार्थतः ।
शुद्धबुद्धस्वरूपस्त्वं मा गमः क्षुद्रचित्तताम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
tvayā vyāptamidaṃ viśvaṃ tvayi protaṃ yathārthataḥ |
śuddhabuddhasvarūpastvaṃ mā gamaḥ kṣudracittatām ||
verses.english: This entire universe is pervaded by you and interwoven in you. You are pure consciousness by nature. Do not fall into a petty, limited state of mind.
verses.commentary: Using the metaphor of thread woven through cloth, the universe isn't separate from you; it is constructed out of your own conscious substance.

understand.common_misconception: Feeling small and isolated inside a massive, cold, and uncaring universe.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that the vast universe arises and takes its shape inside your own wide-open awareness.

verses.philosophy: Awareness is the fundamental fabric of reality. Adopting a limited identity (*Kṣudra-cittatām*) contradicts the boundless nature of the Self.

verses.sanskrit:
निरपेक्षो निर्विकारो निर्भरः शीतलाशयः ।
अगाधबुद्धिसम्पन्नो भव चिन्मात्रवासना ॥
verses.transliteration:
nirapekṣo nirvikāro nirbharaḥ śītalāśayaḥ |
agādhabuddhisampanno bhava cinmātravāsanā ||
verses.english: You are independent, unchanging, absolute, and serene. You are endowed with profound intelligence. Remain centered solely in pure awareness.
verses.commentary: True peace means having a completely cool mind (*śītalāśayaḥ*), untouched by hot desires or reactive emotional swings.

understand.common_misconception: Fearing that spiritual independence will leave you cold, indifferent, or emotionally blank.
understand.attainment_meaning: It brings a stable, deep peace that allows you to engage with life without being thrown off balance by it.

verses.philosophy: When the mind stops seeking external validation, it settles into its natural, unconditioned state (*Chinmatra*).

verses.sanskrit:
साकारमनृतं विद्धि निराकारं तु निश्चलम् ।
एतत्तत्त्वोपदेशेन न पुनर्भवसम्भवः ॥
verses.transliteration:
sākāramanṛtaṃ viddhi nirākāraṃ tu niścalam |
etattattvopadeśena na punarbhavasambhavaḥ ||
verses.english: Know that what has form is false, and only the formless is eternal. Through this direct instruction, you will not fall back into the illusion of birth and death.
verses.commentary: Forms are constantly morphing and changing, which means they lack absolute reality. Only the formless space behind them remains completely steady.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that calling the physical world 'false' means it doesn't exist at all.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means the world is temporary and shifting, like a movie on a screen, whereas your awareness is the solid screen underneath.

verses.philosophy: This is the core distinction between *Drisyadhari* (the changing scene) and *Drashta* (the unchanging witness), which dissolves the fear of mortality.

verses.sanskrit:
यथैव आदर्शमध्यस्थे रूपे अन्तः परितस्तु सः ।
तथैव अस्मिन् शरीरे अन्तः परितः परमेश्वरः ॥
verses.transliteration:
yathaiva ādarśamadhyasthe rūpe antaḥ paritastu saḥ |
tathaiva asmin śarīre antaḥ paritaḥ parameśvaraḥ ||
verses.english: Just as a mirror exists both inside and outside the reflection it shows, the Supreme Self exists both inside and outside this physical body.
verses.commentary: A beautiful mirror metaphor. The images on the glass don't divide, scratch, or alter the quality of the mirror itself; it contains them completely.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that your soul is a small spark locked up inside your heart or brain.
understand.attainment_meaning: Your body is a temporary reflection occurring inside the mirror of your boundless consciousness.

verses.philosophy: Awareness is non-local and continuous. It exists both inside and outside physical forms, rendering boundaries completely illusory.

verses.sanskrit:
एकं सर्वगतं व्योम बहिरन्तर्यथा घटे ।
नित्यं निरन्तरं ब्रह्म सर्वभूतगणे तथा ॥
verses.transliteration:
ekaṃ sarvagataṃ vyoma bahirantaryathā ghaṭe |
nityam nirantaraṃ brahma sarvabhūtagaṇe tathā ||
verses.english: Just as the same space exists inside and outside a clay pot, the eternal, all-pervading Brahman exists continuously within all beings.
verses.commentary: The classic pot-space metaphor (*Ghatakasha*). Breaking the clay pot doesn't shatter, move, or destroy the space inside it; it simply reveals that the inner and outer space were always one.

understand.common_misconception: Worrying that when your physical body dies, your personal consciousness will be destroyed along with it.
understand.attainment_meaning: The drop of the body is just like the breaking of a clay pot—the space inside remains exactly as it always was.

verses.philosophy: Individualized consciousness (*Jivatman*) and universal consciousness (*Paramatman*) are fundamentally identical. The apparent boundary is temporary and nominal.

title: Ascharya Darshanam
description: The Marvel of Infinite Self-Realization
text: This chapter transitions to King Janaka's voice as he speaks from his newfound state of absolute awakening, describing his realization of the infinite, unattached Self.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
अहो निरञ्जनः शान्तः बोधोऽहं प्रकृतेः परः ।
एतावन्तमहं कालं मोहेनैव विडम्बितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
aho nirañjanaḥ śāntaḥ bodho'ham prakṛteḥ paraḥ |
etāvantamahaṃ kālaṃ mohenaiva viḍambitaḥ ||
verses.english: Janaka said: Oh, I am stainless, peaceful, pure awareness, and beyond nature! All this time I have been duped and deluded by illusion.
verses.commentary: Janaka experiences an immediate shift in consciousness. He gasps at his own pristine nature, recognizing that his previous identification with suffering was a trick of the mind.

understand.common_misconception: Most people assume enlightenment requires a long process of cleansing the human ego until it becomes perfect.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realization is realizing the true Self was never dirty, damaged, or affected by the ego's history in the first place.

verses.philosophy: The Self (*Atman*) is inherently *Nirañjana* (stainless) and independent of *Prakriti* (material nature). Delusion is not an entity; it is a temporary lapse in attention.

verses.sanskrit:
यथा प्रकाशयामीदं शरीरमेकं तथा जगत् ।
अतो मम जगत्सर्वं न वा किञ्चन वै मम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā prakāśayāmīdaṃ śarīramekaṃ tathā jagat |
ato mama jagatsarvaṃ na vā kiñcana vai mam ||
verses.english: Just as I illuminate this single body, so do I illuminate the entire universe. Therefore, either the whole world belongs to me, or nothing at all belongs to me.
verses.commentary: Janaka expands his sense of self. He recognizes that the light of consciousness running through his physical form is the very same light illuminating the stars.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that spiritual detachment means feeling disconnected, isolated, or cold toward the world.
understand.attainment_meaning: True detachment means realizing the world is a part of you, which removes the need to possess or control things.

verses.philosophy: The individual witness (*Jiva*) dissolves into the universal witness (*Brahman*). Ownership disappears because there is no separate object left to own.

verses.sanskrit:
सशरीरं जगत् त्यक्त्वा मया कश्चित् कौशलात् ।
अधुना कुत्रचित् कश्चिद् आत्मा आलोक्यते मया ॥
verses.transliteration:
saśarīraṃ jagat tyaktā mayā kaścit kauśalāt |
adhunā kutracit kaścid ātmā ālokyate mayā ||
verses.english: Having abandoned the body along with the world, now, through some incredible grace, the Supreme Self is directly perceived by me.
verses.commentary: Janaka credits his awakening to a sudden intuitive leap (*kauśalāt*) that allows him to step outside the mind's continuous tracking of form.

understand.common_misconception: Believing you must physically destroy your body or wait for death to experience the formless Self.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means dropping the mental habit of using bodily senses as the only judge of what is real.

verses.philosophy: Perceiving the Self is not an external sensory observation. It is a self-luminous recognition where awareness turns around and becomes aware of itself.

verses.sanskrit:
यथा न तोयतो भिन्नास्तरङ्गाः फेनबुद्बुदाः ।
तथा आत्मनो न भिन्नं विश्वमात्मविनिर्गतम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā na toyato bhinnāstaraṅgāḥ phenabudbudāḥ |
tathā ātmano na bhinnaṃ viśvamātmavinirgatam ||
verses.english: Just as waves, foam, and bubbles are not separate from water, so the universe, born from the Self, is not separate from the Self.
verses.commentary: Janaka uses a classic non-dual metaphor. A wave cannot claim to be independent of the ocean; its form, movement, and essence are entirely made of water.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that non-dual philosophy treats the physical world as a complete hallucination with zero value.
understand.attainment_meaning: The world's independent existence is what is false; its essence as pure consciousness is absolutely real.

verses.philosophy: This illustrates *Vivartavada* (the doctrine of apparent transformation). The effect (the universe) is nothing other than its underlying cause (the Self).

verses.sanskrit:
तन्तुमात्रो भवेदेव पटो यद्वद्विचारितः ।
तथा विश्वमिदं सर्वं आत्ममात्रं विचारितम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
tantumātro bhavedeva paṭo yadvadvicāritaḥ |
tathā viśvamidaṃ sarvaṃ ātmamātram vicāritam ||
verses.english: Just as a cloth, when examined closely, is found to be nothing but thread, so this universe, when analyzed, is found to be nothing but the Self.
verses.commentary: If you unravel a shirt completely, the shirt disappears and only thread remains. Janaka notes that looking deeply into any object reveals pure awareness at its core.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that things possess a solid, separate nature from the consciousness perceiving them.
understand.attainment_meaning: Forms are simply temporary arrangements of a single underlying reality.

verses.philosophy: Names and forms (*Nama-Rupa*) are superficial overlays. Real analysis dissolves the overlay to expose the continuous substrate.

verses.sanskrit:
इक्षुरसे ज्ञेया शर्करा तेन निर्मिता ।
यथा तथैव मयि विश्वं मन्मात्रेण प्रपूरितम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
ikṣurase jñeyā śarkarā tena nirmitā |
yathā tathaiva mayi viśvaṃ manmātreṇa prapūritam ||
verses.english: Just as sugar made from sugarcane juice is completely permeated with that sweetness, so the universe produced in me is thoroughly filled with my own nature.
verses.commentary: The sweetness of sugar cannot be isolated from the sugar itself. Janaka realizes his essence permeates every texture of his life experience.

understand.common_misconception: Imagining God or the Absolute as an external creator sitting outside the universe.
understand.attainment_meaning: The universe is built *out* of consciousness, making every atom inherently divine.

verses.philosophy: Consciousness is the material cause (*Upadana*) as well as the intelligent cause (*Nimitta*) of everything that appears.

verses.sanskrit:
आत्माज्ञानाज्जगद्भाति आत्मज्ञानान्न भासते ।
रज्ज्वज्ञानादहिर्भाति तज्ज्ञानाद्भासते न हि ॥
verses.transliteration:
ātmājñānājjagadbhāti ātmajñānānna bhāsate |
rajvvajñānādahirbhāti tajjñānādbhāsate na hi ||
verses.english: The world appears from ignorance of the Self, and disappears when the Self is known. Just as a snake appears when a rope is not recognized, and vanishes once the rope is seen.
verses.commentary: Janaka reinforces Ashtavakra's earlier rope-and-snake lesson. The illusion of a dangerous world vanishes the moment you recognize the underlying reality.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting the physical world to vanish into thin air upon gaining spiritual wisdom.
understand.attainment_meaning: The physical objects remain, but your belief that they are separate, binding realities disappears completely.

verses.philosophy: The world as a source of bondage exists solely due to *Avidya* (ignorance). Correct knowledge (*Jnana*) instantly cancels out the illusion.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रकाशो मे निजं रूपं नातिरिक्तोऽस्म्यहं ततः ।
यदा प्रकाशते विश्वं तदाहंभास एव हि ॥
verses.transliteration:
prakāśo me nijaṃ rūpaṃ nātirikto'smyahaṃ tataḥ |
yadā prakāśate viśvaṃ tadāhaṃbhāsa eva hi ||
verses.english: Light is my fundamental nature; I am nothing other than that light. When the universe shines, it is simply my own light shining forth.
verses.commentary: You don't *have* a soul; you *are* the light that makes experience possible. Janaka recognizes that everything he sees is a reflection of his own awareness.

understand.common_misconception: Viewing consciousness as a byproduct of brain activity or a tool used by the mind.
understand.attainment_meaning: Consciousness is the primary light source, and the mind and brain are simply instruments illuminated by it.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Sva-Prakasha* (self-luminous). It does not need another light source to be seen, because it is the source of all illumination.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो कल्पितमहो मयि अज्ञानतो जगत् ।
यथा शुक्तौ रजतं मरुभूमौ जलं मरिच्यपि ॥
verses.transliteration:
aho kalpitamaho mayi ajñānato jagat |
yathā śuktau rajataṃ marubhūmau jalaṃ maricyapi ||
verses.english: Wonderful! The world appears in me through ignorance, like silver in mother-of-pearl, a mirage oasis in the desert sand, or a phantom in the twilight.
verses.commentary: Janaka marvels at how easily the mind gets fooled. Just as sunlight hitting desert sand creates the illusion of water, ignorance creates the illusion of a world separate from the Self.

understand.common_misconception: Blaming the world or other people for causing your inner suffering and distress.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing your suffering comes entirely from misinterpreting what you see, not from the things themselves.

verses.philosophy: The universe has an overlay status (*Adhyasa*). It borrows its apparent reality from the true Self, just as a mirage borrows its appearance from the desert sand.

verses.sanskrit:
मत्तो विनिर्गतं विश्वं मय्येव लयमेष्यति ।
मृदीव घटो जलधौ तरङ्गः कनके कटकं यथा ॥
verses.transliteration:
matto vinirgataṃ viśvaṃ mayyeva layameṣyati |
mṛdīva ghaṭo jaladhau taraṅgaḥ kanake kaṭakaṃ yathā ||
verses.english: The universe arises from me and dissolves back into me, just as a clay pot returns to clay, a wave returns to the ocean, or a gold bracelet melts back into gold.
verses.commentary: Forms are temporary shapes assumed by a single material. A gold bracelet can be melted down and reshaped into a ring, but the gold itself never changes.

understand.common_misconception: Viewing death as a terrifying void that completely destroys your existence.
understand.attainment_meaning: Death is simply a change of form, like a wave settling back into the stillness of the ocean.

verses.philosophy: The cycles of creation (*Srishti*) and dissolution (*Laya*) occur entirely within absolute awareness, which remains unchanged throughout both.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो अहं नमस्तुभ्यं विनाशो यस्य नास्ति मे ।
ब्रह्मादिस्तम्बपर्यन्तजगन्नाशेऽपि तिष्ठतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
aho ahaṃ namastubhyaṃ vināśo yasya nāsti me |
brahmādistambaparyantajagannāśe'pi tiṣṭhataḥ ||
verses.english: Wonderful am I! Salutations to myself, who knows no destruction. I remain untouched even if the entire universe, from Brahma down to a blade of grass, is destroyed.
verses.commentary: Janaka bows inward to his true identity. This is not arrogance; it is the realization that your fundamental awareness cannot be burned, cut, or destroyed by any worldly event.

understand.common_misconception: Confusing this self-salutation with egoistic vanity or personal pride.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is bowing to the infinite space of awareness that remains after the personal ego has been dropped.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Nitya* (eternal) and *Achyuta* (imperishable). It is the unmoving background that outlasts all cosmic transformations.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो अहं नमस्तुभ्यं एकोऽहं देहवानपि ।
क्वचिन्न गन्ता नागन्ता व्याप्य विश्वमवस्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
aho ahaṃ namastubhyaṃ eko'haṃ dehavānapi |
kvacinna gantā nāgantā vyāpya viśvamavasthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Wonderful am I! Salutations to myself! Though appearing to inhabit a body, I am completely one. I neither go anywhere nor come from anywhere, but pervade the entire universe.
verses.commentary: The mind thinks the soul moves when the body walks across a room. Janaka corrects this: the body moves *within* space, and space moves *within* your infinite awareness.

understand.common_misconception: Believing your consciousness is a localized energy trapped inside your skull that travels with you when you move.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing you are the stationary space in which your body's movements are tracked.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Sarvagata* (all-pervading) and motionless. Movement is a physical attribute; it does not apply to the spacious witness.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो अहं नमस्तुभ्यं दळोऽस्ति न हि मत्समः ।
असंस्पृश्य शरीरेण येन विश्वं चिरं धृतम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
aho ahaṃ namastubhyaṃ daḷo'sti na hi matsamaḥ |
asaṃspṛśya śarīreṇa yena viśvaṃ ciraṃ dhṛtam ||
verses.english: Wonderful am I! Salutations to myself! There is no one as capable as I, who bears the weight of the universe forever without even touching it with a body.
verses.commentary: Janaka uses a paradox: awareness holds up the entire world of experience without using any physical effort, just like a screen holds a movie without touching the actors.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that maintaining an enlightened state requires constant mental effort or concentration.
understand.attainment_meaning: Your true nature holds everything effortlessly, without needing any management systems.

verses.philosophy: The Absolute supports the world without any physical connection (*Asanga*). It is the ground of reality, yet it remains completely unpolluted by it.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो अहं नमस्तुभ्यं यस्य मे नास्ति किञ्चन ।
अथवा यस्य मे सर्वं यद्वाङ्मनसगोचरम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
aho ahaṃ namastubhyaṃ yasya me nāsti kiñcana |
athavā yasya me sarvaṃ yadvāṅmanasagocaram ||
verses.english: Wonderful am I! Salutations to myself! I possess nothing at all, or I possess everything that can be reached by speech and thought.
verses.commentary: Janaka highlights a beautiful truth: by dropping the desire to own specific things, you realize you are the very space in which everything exists.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that spiritual freedom requires you to give up your possessions and live in poverty.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means giving up the illusion of personal ownership, which connects you back to the wholeness of life.

verses.philosophy: The Self is simultaneously *Sunya* (free of any objects) and *Purna* (the fullness of everything), moving beyond standard categories of having or lacking.

verses.sanskrit:
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं तथा ज्ञाता त्रितयं नास्ति वास्तवम् ।
अज्ञानाद्भाति यत्रेदं सोऽहमस्मि निरञ्जनः ॥
verses.transliteration:
jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ tathā jñātā tritayaṃ nāsti vāstavam |
ajñānādbhāti yatredaṃ so'hamasmi nirañjanaḥ ||
verses.english: Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower—this triad does not exist in reality. I am that stainless awareness in which this illusion appears.
verses.commentary: Janaka dissolves the basic structure of human thought: the split between the person looking (*knower*), the act of looking (*knowledge*), and the thing looked at (*known*).

understand.common_misconception: Treating enlightenment as a special piece of information or an object that a seeker can acquire.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that when the seeking mind settles down, the separate seeker and the sought merge back into one.

verses.philosophy: This is the collapse of the *Triputi* (the triple duality). Pure consciousness is a single reality that looks like a split perspective only when filtered through the mind.

verses.sanskrit:
द्वैतमूलमहो दुःखं नान्यत्तस्यास्ति भेषजम् ।
दृश्यमेतदसत्यं हि एकोऽहं चिद्रसोऽमलः ॥
verses.transliteration:
dvaitamūlamaho duḥkhaṃ nānyattasyāsti bheṣajam |
dṛśyametadasatyaṃ hi eko'haṃ cidraso'malaḥ ||
verses.english: Oh, the root of suffering is duality! There is no cure for it other than realizing that everything perceived is unreal, and that I am the single, pure essence.
verses.commentary: Believing that you are separate from life creates comparison, fear, and a constant search for security. Janaka notes that the only permanent cure is dropping this sense of separation.

understand.common_misconception: Trying to fix psychological suffering by changing your job, your relationship, or your life conditions.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that while you can improve your conditions, true peace comes from changing your identification from the scene to the witness.

verses.philosophy: Duality (*Dvaita*) is the sole source of psychological fear (*Bhaya*). True liberation is the resting of awareness in its own non-dual essence.

verses.sanskrit:
बोधमात्रोऽहमेवास्मि अज्ञानादुपाधिः कल्पितो मया ।
एवं विमृशतो नित्यं निर्विकल्पे स्थितिर्मम ॥
verses.transliteration:
bodhamātro'hameva'smi ajñānādupādhiḥ kalpito mayā |
evaṃ vimṛśato nityaṃ nirvikalpe sthitirmama ||
verses.english: I am pure awareness alone. Through ignorance, I have imagined artificial limitations for myself. By resting continuously on this truth, I remain in the unconditioned state.
verses.commentary: The mind uses filters or *Upadhis* (like 'I am a manager,' 'I am old,' 'I am anxious'). Janaka steps back from these filters, recognizing them as passing thoughts.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that your limiting mental habits are permanent parts of your identity.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that limitations are just commentary running in your mind, while your underlying awareness remains free.

verses.philosophy: An *Upadhi* is a limiting conditioning mechanism. Shifting focus away from these concepts naturally allows awareness to settle into its unconditioned state (*Nirvikalpa*).

verses.sanskrit:
न मे बन्धोऽस्ति मोक्षो वा भ्रान्तिः शान्ता निराश्रया ।
अहो मयि जगद्रूपं विद्यते न परमार्थतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
na me bandho'sti mokṣo vā bhrāntiḥ śāntā nirāśrayā |
aho mayi jagadrūpaṃ vidyate na paramārthataḥ ||
verses.english: I have neither bondage nor liberation. The illusion has lost its footing and vanished. Oh, the universe exists in me, but in reality, it does not exist at all.
verses.commentary: If bondage is an illusion, then looking for liberation is also unnecessary. Janaka realizes that searching for freedom keeps the false idea that you are trapped alive.

understand.common_misconception: Spending years trying to clear out personal karma or fix a trapped soul.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing your true nature was never trapped, which causes the entire search to drop away naturally.

verses.philosophy: This is the ultimate perspective of *Ajatavada* (the doctrine of non-origination). From the absolute viewpoint, no creation, no bondage, and no liberation ever took place.

verses.sanskrit:
सशरीरमिदं विश्वं न किञ्चिदिति निश्चितम् ।
शुद्धचिन्मात्रआत्मा च तत्कस्मिन कल्पना अधुना ॥
verses.transliteration:
saśarīramidaṃ viśvaṃ na kiñciditi niścitam |
śuddhacinmātraātmā ca tatkasmin kalpanā adhunā ||
verses.english: Now that I know for certain that this body and the universe are nothing in themselves, and that the Self is pure consciousness alone, what room is there for imagination?
verses.commentary: When you see through a magic trick, you can't be fooled by it again. Janaka's mind stops spinning stories because he is anchored in what is real.

understand.common_misconception: Trying to silence your thoughts through force or mental discipline.
understand.attainment_meaning: Thoughts naturally quiet down when you see they don't define who you are.

verses.philosophy: When your identification shifts to pure awareness (*Chinmatra*), the mind's habit of projecting conceptual stories (*Vikalpa*) naturally runs out of fuel.

verses.sanskrit:
शरीरं स्वर्गनरकौ बन्धमोक्षौ भयं तथा ।
कल्पनामात्रमेवैतत् किं मे कार्यं चिदात्मनः ॥
verses.transliteration:
śarīraṃ svarganarakau bandhamokṣau bhayaṃ tathā |
kalpanāmātramevaitat kiṃ me kāryaṃ cidātmanaḥ ||
verses.english: The body, heaven and hell, bondage and liberation, as well as fear—these are all mere projections of the mind. What have I to do with any of them, I who am pure consciousness?
verses.commentary: Janaka closes the chapter by dismissing all mental categories. Heaven, hell, success, and failure are just concepts passing through your awareness, leaving your true identity untouched.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that spiritual peace is a safe emotional haven where bad things are blocked out.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is realizing you are the vast space that contains both good and bad experiences without being defined by them.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Krita-Kritya* (having accomplished all that is to be done). It is completely free from duties, rules, or the need to chase specific mental experiences.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो जनसमूहेऽपि न द्वैत पश्यतो मम ।
अरण्यमिव संवृत्तं क्व रतिं करवाण्यहम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
ahō janasamūhē'pi na dvaita paśyatō mama |
aranyamiva saṃvṛttaṃ kva ratiṃ karavāṇyaham ||
verses.english: Oh, for me who sees no duality even in the midst of a crowd, it is as if it has turned into a solitary desert forest. Where can I find attachment or aversion?
verses.commentary: Janaka looks out at his royal court and bustling kingdom. Because he no longer sees people as separate entities threatening or feeding his ego, a crowded city feels just as peaceful as a quiet forest.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that an enlightened person must physically isolate themselves from cities and society to stay in a peaceful state.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that when the mind stops projecting personal desires and judgments onto people, public crowds can no longer disturb your inner solitude.

verses.philosophy: When non-dual vision (*Advaita Drishti*) is fully integrated, the false distinction between a 'distracting marketplace' and a 'quiet hermitage' disappears entirely.

verses.sanskrit:
नाहं देहो न मे देहो जीवो नाहमहं हि चित् ।
अयमेव हि मे बन्ध आसीद्या जीविते स्पृहा ॥
verses.transliteration:
nāhaṃ dēhō na mē dēhō jīvō nāhamahaṃ hi chit |
ayamēva hi mē bandha āsīdyā jīvitē spṛhā ||
verses.english: I am not the body, nor is the body mine. I am not the individualized soul; I am pure consciousness. My only true bondage was my desperate clinging to physical life.
verses.commentary: Janaka tracks down the root of all existential anxiety: the biological survival mechanism's desperate craving to protect its temporary identity at all costs.

understand.common_misconception: Fearing that losing your 'clinging to life' means you will instantly become suicidal or neglect your health.
understand.attainment_meaning: It simply means dropping the constant, exhausting psychological terror of aging and physical death, allowing you to live fully.

verses.philosophy: The ultimate knot of ego is *Abhinivesha* (the instinctual fear of death). Realizing your nature is pure awareness (*Chit*) breaks this biological trap instantly.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो भुवनकल्लोलैर्विचित्रैर्द्राक् समुत्थितम् ।
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ चित्तवाते समुद्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
ahō bhuvanakallōlairvicitrairdrāk samutthitam |
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau cittavātē samudyatē ||
verses.english: Oh, in the infinite ocean of myself, when the winds of the mind blow, the colorful waves of diverse worlds instantly arise!
verses.commentary: Janaka introduces a brilliant cosmic ocean metaphor. The thoughts inside the mind act like winds that whip up the still surface of consciousness into the diverse movements of a physical universe.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that the physical universe is an independent, solid object sitting completely outside of your awareness.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that the entire world of form is a dynamic wave pattern taking shape directly inside your vast field of experience.

verses.philosophy: This is a direct expression of *Drishti-Srishti-Vada* (the philosophy that perception and creation occur simultaneously). The universe is consciousness in motion.

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ चित्तवाते प्रशाम्यति ।
अभाग्याज्जीववणिजो जगत्पोतो विनश्वरः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau cittavātē praśāmyati |
abhāgyājjīvavaṇijō jagatpōtō vinaśvaraḥ ||
verses.english: When the wind of the mind falls calm in the infinite ocean of myself, the trading boat of the individual seeker unfortunately sinks and breaks apart.
verses.commentary: Janaka uses poetic irony. The 'trading boat' is the ego's spiritual journey of collecting good deeds, meditation experiences, and credits. When the mind goes perfectly quiet, that separate seeker evaporates into the absolute ocean.

understand.common_misconception: Hoping that enlightenment will turn your individual ego into a permanent, legendary spiritual superhero.
understand.attainment_meaning: Enlightenment is the total dissolution of the separate ego container, leaving only the boundless ocean behind.

verses.philosophy: The *Jiva* (individual traveler) relies completely on mental activity to maintain its boundaries. When the mind settles into absolute stillness, the illusion of separation cannot survive.

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधावाश्चर्यं जीववीचयः ।
उद्यन्ति घ्नन्ति खेलन्ति प्रविशन्ति स्वभावतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhāvāścaryaṃ jīvavīcayaḥ |
udyanti ghnanti khēlanti praviśanti svabhāvataḥ ||
verses.english: How wonderful! In the infinite ocean of myself, the waves of individual living beings naturally arise, collide, play, and dissolve back into each other according to their own nature.
verses.commentary: Janaka closes Chapter 2 with an exquisite, compassionate overview of existence. From his place of deep peace, the births, struggles, interactions, and deaths of people are seen simply as the harmless, natural movement of waves playing on the surface of an infinite sea.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting spiritual awakening to freeze the world into static perfection where nothing ever changes, decays, or passes away.
understand.attainment_meaning: Watching the natural cycles of life, growth, and decay unfold around you without losing your internal anchor in the unchanging background.

verses.philosophy: This is the state of *Jivanmukti* (liberation while living). The universe is no longer feared as a trap of bondage; it is celebrated as the spontaneous, beautiful play (*Lila*) of your own infinite awareness.

title: Adhyatma Upadesha
description: The Test of True Self-Awareness
text: In this chapter, the tone shifts back to the sage Ashtavakra. Having heard King Janaka’s ecstatic declarations of enlightenment in Chapter 2, Ashtavakra tests the depth of Janaka's realization. He warns him that if he truly knows himself as the infinite, all-pervading Self, he can no longer live like an ordinary, desire-driven worldly ego chasing sensory pleasures or wealth.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
अविनाशिनमात्मानं एकं विज्ञाय तत्त्वतः ।
तवात्मज्ञस्य धीरस्य कथमर्थार्जने रतिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
avināśinamātmānaṃ ekaṃ vijñāya tattvataḥ |
tavātmajñasya dhīrasya kathamarthārjane ratiḥ ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: Knowing the Self as truly one and indestructible, how can you, a wise person settled in self-knowledge, still find pleasure in chasing wealth?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra applies a direct stress-test to Janaka's realization. Intellectual comprehension of non-duality is easy, but if the heart still hungers for worldly accumulation, the ego is still running the show.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that spiritual realization can simply be an intellectual theory or philosophy that has zero impact on your daily desires and actions.
understand.attainment_meaning: True insight naturally changes what you value, dissolving the desperate, anxious drive to accumulate material things for self-worth.

verses.philosophy: This addresses the necessity of alignment between *Jnana* (knowledge) and *Vairagya* (dispassion). If the Self is recognized as complete, seeking external addition is a logical contradiction.

verses.sanskrit:
आत्माज्ञानादहो प्रीतिर्विषयभ्रमगोचरे ।
शुक्रेरजताज्ञानं लोभ उत्पद्यते यथा ॥
verses.transliteration:
ātmājñānādahō prītirviṣayabhramagōcarē |
śukterajatājñānaṃ lōbha utpadyatē yathā ||
verses.english: Alas, attachment to sensual objects arises only from ignorance of the Self, just as greed for silver arises from mistaking mother-of-pearl for it.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra explains why people chase temporary pleasures. A person who mistakes a shiny seashell for precious silver will fight to possess it. Once they see it is just a shell, the greed evaporates instantly.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must violently suppress your desires or punish your senses to be spiritual.
understand.attainment_meaning: When you look deeply at what you are chasing and realize it cannot offer permanent fulfillment, the desire falls away on its own.

verses.philosophy: Desire (*Kama*) relies entirely on a distorted perception of value (*Adhyasa*). Correcting the perception destroys the motivational root of the desire.

verses.sanskrit:
विश्वं स्फुरति यत्रेदं तरङ्गा इव सागरे ।
सोऽहमस्मीति विज्ञाय किं दीन इव धावसि ॥
verses.transliteration:
viśvaṃ sphurati yatrēdaṃ taraṅgā iva sāgarē |
sō'hamasmīti vijñāya kiṃ dīna iva dhāvasi ||
verses.english: Knowing yourself to be that infinite consciousness in which the universe vibrates like waves in the ocean, why do you run about like a pathetic beggar?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra uses an intense confrontation. If you are the ocean containing all of existence, running after a tiny drop of worldly validation or sensory delight is an act of self-beggary.

understand.common_misconception: Feeling like an incomplete person who needs to find the right relationship, job, or status to finally be whole.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing you are the absolute space hosting all experiences, meaning you are already fundamentally complete.

verses.philosophy: The identity of the individual is *Purna* (the fullness). Running after objects implies a sense of lack (*Apurnata*), which is the core illusion of the ego.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रुत्वापि शुद्धचैतन्यं आत्मानमतिसुन्दरम् ।
उपस्थे रतिमाप्नोति मालिन्यायै प्रकृष्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
śrutvāpi śuddhacaitanyaṃ ātmānamatisundaram |
upasthē ratimāpnōti mālinyāyai prakṛṣyatē ||
verses.english: Even after hearing that the Self is pure, untainted, and supremely beautiful, how can one still fall back into sensory lust and become degraded by base desires?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra targets the stickiness of biological conditioning. He wonders how awareness, once touched by its own pristine nature, can let itself get dragged back into short-term physical impulses.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that hearing a spiritual truth once is enough to completely clean out old, deeply ingrained bodily habits.
understand.attainment_meaning: Real wisdom requires constant awareness to ensure old mental tracks don't quietly hijack your state of mind again.

verses.philosophy: This deals with *Vasanas*—the latent, habitual tendencies of the mind-body instrument that persist even after intellectual exposure to higher truths.

verses.sanskrit:
सर्वभूतेषु चात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि ।
मुनेर्जानत आश्चर्यं ममत्वमनुवर्तते ॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūtēṣu cātmānaṃ sarvabhūtāni cātmani |
munērjānata āścaryaṃ mamatvamanuvartatē ||
verses.english: It is strange indeed that a sage who knows the Self is in all beings, and all beings are in the Self, should still retain any sense of exclusive personal possession!
verses.commentary: If everything is an expression of one shared consciousness, claiming 'this belongs to me and not to you' makes no sense. Ashtavakra exposes the lingering traces of possessiveness.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking you can live in absolute oneness during meditation, while remaining completely greedy and selfish in your daily relationships.
understand.attainment_meaning: True non-duality melts the hard boundaries of personal ownership, paving the way for unconditional sharing.

verses.philosophy: The illusion of *Mamata* (mine-ness) is an extension of the primary illusion of *Ahamkara* (the separate ego). One cannot collapse without the other.

verses.sanskrit:
आस्थितः परमाद्वैतं मोक्षार्थेऽपि व्यवस्थितः ।
आश्चर्यं कामवशगो विकलः केलिकर्मभिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
āsthitaḥ paramādvaitaṃ mōkṣārthē'pi vyavasthitaḥ |
āścaryaṃ kāmavaśagō vikalaḥ kēlikarmabhiḥ ||
verses.english: It is astonishing that one anchored in the highest non-duality, fully intent on absolute freedom, should still be destabilized by lustful play and games.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra highlights the contradiction of being an absolute seeker who still gets easily distracted by trivial social dramas and transient physical thrills.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you can master non-dual philosophy while letting your sensory whims dictate your actual focus and time.
understand.attainment_meaning: True freedom means having a mind that doesn't get swept away by every passing temptation or worldly distraction.

verses.philosophy: Resting in the unconditioned state requires absolute mental integrity. Becoming a slave to impulses (*Kama*) fractures your presence and pulls you back into duality.

verses.sanskrit:
उद्भूतं ज्ञानदुर्मित्रं अवधार्यातिदुर्बलः ।
आश्चर्यं काममाकाङ्क्षेत् कालमन्तमुपस्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
udbhūtaṃ jñānadurmitraṃ avadhāryātidurbalaḥ |
āścaryaṃ kāmamākāṅkhēt kālamantamupasthitaḥ ||
verses.english: It is bizarre that a person who understands that desire is the ultimate enemy of wisdom should, even when facing death, still crave sensory gratification.
verses.commentary: Time is running out for the mortal body. Ashtavakra points out how absurd it is to spend your remaining life force chasing temporary comforts that will vanish anyway.

understand.common_misconception: Procrastinating on your inner growth, assuming you will naturally become wise and peaceful when you get old.
understand.attainment_meaning: If you don't intentionally break your addiction to distractions right now, those cravings will follow you all the way to your final breath.

verses.philosophy: Desire is termed *Jnana-Durmitra* (the enemy of wisdom). It clouds clear judgment, tricking the individual into prioritizing short-term sensations over eternal reality.

verses.sanskrit:
इहामुत्र विरक्तस्य नित्यानित्यविवेकिनः ।
आश्चर्यं मोक्षकामस्य मोक्षादेव विभीषिका ॥
verses.transliteration:
ihāmutra viraktasya nityānityavivēkinaḥ |
āścaryaṃ mōkṣakāmasya mōkṣādēva vibhīṣikā ||
verses.english: It is fascinating that one who is detached from both this world and the next, who can tell the eternal from the temporary, should still be terrified of ultimate liberation!
verses.commentary: This highlights a deep psychological paradox: many people say they want complete freedom, but they are terrified of what it actually takes—the total dissolution of their personal ego.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting enlightenment to be a cozy reward that makes your personal ego live happily ever after.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is the peaceful exit of the ego altogether. True freedom can feel scary to a mind that is still addicted to control.

verses.philosophy: The ego experiences liberation as its own death. Passing through this fear requires total surrender, letting go of your separate identity.

verses.sanskrit:
धीरस्तु भोज्यमानोऽपि पीड्यमानोऽपि सर्वदा ।
आत्मानं केवलं पश्यन् न तुष्यति न कुप्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
dhīrastu bhōjyamānō'pi pīḍyamānō'pi sarvadā |
ātmānaṃ kēvalaṃ paśyan na tuṣyati na kupyati ||
verses.english: The truly wise person, whether feasting on luxury or suffering pain, always sees only the solitary Self. They neither celebrate nor get angry.
verses.commentary: Here is the blueprint of an integrated sage. Outer conditions will change—sometimes life brings a feast, sometimes it brings hardship—but the witness remains steady and unbothered through both.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that a spiritual person lives a charmed life where physical pain or unpleasant situations never happen.
understand.attainment_meaning: Life’s ups and downs still happen, but you stop letting them dictate your internal peace and balance.

verses.philosophy: This describes *Samatvam* (equanimity). The sage is anchored in the *Kevala* (the absolute, unattached witness), keeping them completely independent of changing circumstances.

verses.sanskrit:
चेष्टमानं शरीरं स्वं पश्यत्यन्यशरीरवत् ।
संस्तवे च निन्दायां कथं क्षुभ्येत् महाशयः ॥
verses.transliteration:
cēṣṭamānaṃ śarīraṃ svaṃ paśyatyanyaśarīravat |
saṃstavē ca nindāyāṃ kathaṃ kṣubhyēt mahāśayaḥ ||
verses.english: The noble soul watches their own body perform actions as if it were someone else's body. How then can they be shaken by praise or blame?
verses.commentary: When people praise or insult you, they are just making sounds directed at your temporary human persona. A great soul steps back, watching their own body interact as if watching a character in a play.

understand.common_misconception: Becoming an emotional roller-coaster, feeling amazing when praised and devastated when criticized.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that public opinions are just passing weather patterns in other people's minds, completely separate from who you really are.

verses.philosophy: This is total freedom from *Ego-Involvement*. The body acts out its destiny (*Prarabdha*), while your consciousness remains steady, untouched by worldly reviews.

verses.sanskrit:
मायामात्रमिदं विश्वं पश्यन् विगतकौतुकः ।
अपि संनिहिते मृत्यौ कथं त्रस्यति धीरधीः ॥
verses.transliteration:
māyāmātramidaṃ viśvaṃ paśyan vigatakautukaḥ |
api saṃnihitē mṛtyau kathaṃ trasyati dhīradhīḥ ||
verses.english: Seeing this universe as nothing but an illusion, the steady mind loses all obsessive curiosity toward it. How can they feel fear, even with death standing right in front of them?
verses.commentary: When you know the world is a temporary illusion (*Maya*), you stop anxiously tracking its every move. Even physical death loses its teeth because you know your core awareness cannot be deleted.

understand.common_misconception: Living in constant anxiety about the future, checking your health and security every hour of the day.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the panic by realizing that your physical form is temporary, while the space holding your life is immortal.

verses.philosophy: Recognizing the world is *Mithya* (dependently real) cures the mind of worldly obsession (*Vigata-Kautuka*), which instantly removes the root of fear.

verses.sanskrit:
निःस्पृहं मानसं यस्य नैराश्येऽपि महात्मनः ।
तस्यात्मज्ञानतृप्तस्य तुलना केन जायते ॥
verses.transliteration:
niḥspṛhaṃ mānasaṃ yasya nairāśyē'pi mahātmanaḥ |
tasyātmajñānatṛptasya tulanā kēna jāyatē ||
verses.english: Who can be compared to that great soul whose mind is entirely free from desire, who is content even in the absence of hope, and who is fully satisfied in self-knowledge?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra describes an absolute state of peace. When your satisfaction comes from the very fact that you are aware, you no longer need external events to turn out a certain way to feel happy.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that living 'without hope' means being depressed, cynical, or giving up on life.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means you are so deeply happy right now that you don't need to gamble your peace on future promises.

verses.philosophy: This is *Atma-Tripti* (satisfaction in the Self alone). It frees you from the endless loop of setting conditions for your own happiness.

verses.sanskrit:
जानन्नपि न जानाति पश्यन्नपि न पश्यति ।
बुवन्नपि न च ब्रूते कस्येयमुपमा भवेत् ॥
verses.transliteration:
jānannapi na jānāti paśyannapi na paśyati |
bruvannapi na ca brūtē kasyēyamupamā bhavēt ||
verses.english: Knowing, he does not know; seeing, he does not see; speaking, he does not speak. To whom can such a unique soul be compared?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra captures the effortless nature of an enlightened life. The sage functions normally in the world—talking, looking, thinking—but because they don't have a separate ego claiming those actions, they remain completely still inside.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting an enlightened person to act like a rigid stone statue that never speaks or opens its eyes.
understand.attainment_meaning: They live naturally and fluidly, engaging with the world while remaining totally unattached behind the scenes.

verses.philosophy: The sage operates from a state of *Sahaja Avastha* (the natural state). Actions flow smoothly through the body-mind mechanism without leaving any psychological footprints or ego karma.

verses.sanskrit:
भिक्षुर्वा भूपतिर्वापि यो निष्कामः स शोभते ।
भावेषु गतासङ्गः कस्यचित् उत्तमः स्मृतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
bhikṣurvā bhūpatirvāpi yō niṣkāmaḥ sa śōbhatē |
bhāvēṣu gatāsaṅgaḥ kasyacit uttamaḥ smṛtaḥ ||
verses.english: Whether a penniless beggar or a powerful king, glorious is the one who is completely desireless and free from attachment to the world.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra closes his test by leveling all social positions. A king on a throne and a beggar on the street are exactly equal if both are free from internal dependency. True nobility is measured by your inner freedom.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must change your bank account, drop your job, or alter your clothing to find your true self.
understand.attainment_meaning: Your external role is just a costume. True freedom is an inside job, determined by whether your awareness is independent of the role you play.

verses.philosophy: The text lands on *Nishkama* (desirelessness) as the ultimate equalizer. Outer conditions belong to the body's environment; inner freedom belongs exclusively to the unattached witness.

title: Janaka Svasvarupa
description: The Glory of the Awakened Self
text: In this brief but incredibly potent chapter, King Janaka responds to Ashtavakra's intense test from Chapter 3. Instead of becoming defensive, Janaka speaks directly from his lived experience of absolute awakening. He confirms that when a person realizes they are the infinite ocean of awareness, the entire world of comparison, spiritual rules, and mental anxieties naturally falls away.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
हन्तात्मज्ञानस्य धीरस्य खेलतो भोगलीलया ।
न हि संसारवाहकैः मूढैः साम्यं प्रदृश्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
hantātmajñānasya dhīrasya khēlatō bhōgalīlayā |
na hi saṃsāravāhakaiḥ mūḍhaiḥ sāmyaṃ pradṛṣyatē ||
verses.english: Janaka said: Truly, the wise one who knows the Self plays the game of life with effortless joy. There is absolutely no comparison between them and the deluded worldlings who carry the heavy burdens of existence.
verses.commentary: Janaka directly addresses Ashtavakra's warning about worldly actions. He explains that an enlightened sage doesn't interact with the world out of anxiety, need, or greed; they engage with life as a completely free, lighthearted game (*leela*).

understand.common_misconception: Believing that an enlightened person must act heavy, overly serious, or completely detached from any enjoyment in life.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means you can enjoy life's moments fully precisely because you aren't trying to possess them to feel whole.

verses.philosophy: This introduces *Jivanmukta Ananda*—the joy of being liberated while alive. Actions shift from being an anxious attempt to gain happiness (*Bhoga*) to an effortless celebration of inherent completeness (*Lila*).

verses.sanskrit:
यत् पदं प्रेप्सवो दीनाः शक्राद्याः सर्वदेवताः ।
अहो तत्र स्थितो योगी न हर्षमुपगच्छति ॥
verses.transliteration:
yat padaṃ prēpsavō dīnāḥ śakrādyāḥ sarvadēvatāḥ |
ahō tatra sthitō yōgī na harṣamupagacchati ||
verses.english: Oh, the yogi who rests in that supreme state which even Indra and all the other gods desperately beg for, does not feel any personal pride or excitement about it.
verses.commentary: Janaka points out that worldly and heavenly hierarchies are completely trivial. Ordinary minds dream of reaching high positions, power, or heavenly states. A sage rests in the consciousness that hosts all those states, yet feels no egoistic pride about it.

understand.common_misconception: Treating spiritual awakening as a supernatural trophy or badge of superiority to boast about over others.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that true awakening is so natural and complete that it leaves no separate ego behind to feel arrogant or boastful.

verses.philosophy: The state of the Self is *Atitya*—it transcends all relative planes of existence, including heavenly worlds. Because there is no second entity left to compare against, personal excitement (*Harsha*) naturally dissolves.

verses.sanskrit:
तस्य धर्मोऽधर्मो वा क्व च बन्धः क्व मुक्तता ।
यस्य भाति तथा विश्वं न किञ्चिदिव निश्चितम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
tasya dharmō'dharmō vā kva ca bandhaḥ kva muktatā |
yasya bhāti tathā viśvaṃ na kiñcidiva niścitam ||
verses.english: For one who knows for certain that this universe is an illusion and has no independent reality, what meaning is left in virtue or vice, bondage or liberation?
verses.commentary: Janaka leaps past conventional boundaries. If the separate ego is seen as a complete fiction, then the rules designed to govern and free that ego (like moral guilt, spiritual credits, or tracking progress) lose their absolute grip.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that this deep freedom gives a person permission to act cruelly, destructively, or maliciously.
understand.attainment_meaning: A sage doesn't practice kindness to score spiritual points; kindness flows from them naturally because they see everyone as an expression of themselves.

verses.philosophy: This is the transcendence of *Dharmadharma* (good and bad karma). Rules are necessary navigation maps for a mind caught in duality, but they dissolve once you arrive at the non-dual source.

verses.sanskrit:
विश्वं स्फुरति यत्रेदं तरङ्गा इव सागरे ।
सोऽहमस्मीति विज्ञाय किं दीन इव धावसि ॥
verses.transliteration:
viśvaṃ sphurati yatrēdaṃ taraṅgā iva sāgarē |
sō'hamasmīti vijñāya kiṃ dīna iva dhāvasi ||
verses.english: Knowing yourself to be that infinite consciousness in which the universe vibrates like waves in the ocean, why do you run about like a pathetic beggar?
verses.commentary: Janaka echoes Ashtavakra's exact words back to him from verse 3.3. By repeating this phrase, Janaka shows he didn't just hear the teaching intellectually—he has fully digested and merged with it.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that spiritual realization requires you to constantly memorize new, complex theories every day.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is about deeply absorbing and living a few simple, profound truths until they become your natural way of being.

verses.philosophy: This repetition highlights *Nididhyasana*—the stage where a profound non-dual truth moves from an intellectual concept into an undeniable, lived reality.

verses.sanskrit:
श्रुत्वापि शुद्धचैतन्यं आत्मानमतिसुन्दरम् ।
उपस्थे रतिमाप्नोति मालिन्यायै प्रकृष्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
śrutvāpi śuddhacaitanyaṃ ātmānamatisundaram |
upasthē ratimāpnōti mālinyāyai prakṛṣyatē ||
verses.english: Even after hearing that the Self is pure, untainted, and supremely beautiful, how can one still fall back into sensory lust and become degraded by base desires?
verses.commentary: Janaka quotes Ashtavakra's words from verse 3.4 back to him, but speaks from the perspective of an absolute witness. He looks back at the mechanics of human distraction with complete, objective clarity, recognizing that he is entirely free from those old hooks.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you can suppress or delete the human body's basic biological mechanisms through sheer force.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means the body's mechanisms may continue to tick in the background, but they lose all power to confuse or drag down your awareness.

verses.philosophy: The sage is *Sva-Svasthata*—completely centered in his own true nature. He can observe the functional conditioning of the mind-body vehicle without being pulled out of his steady presence.

verses.sanskrit:
सर्वभूतेषु चात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि ।
मुनेर्जानत आश्चर्यं ममत्वमनुवर्तते ॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūtēṣu cātmānaṃ sarvabhūtāni cātmani |
munērjānata āścaryaṃ mamatvamanuvartatē ||
verses.english: It is strange indeed that a sage who knows the Self is in all beings, and all beings are in the Self, should still retain any sense of exclusive personal possession!
verses.commentary: Janaka concludes the chapter by using Ashtavakra's final test parameter (verse 3.5) as his own shield of victory. He confirms that his sense of private ownership has vanished. When you realize you are the space containing everything, the very idea of 'me versus mine' becomes completely obsolete.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that dropping 'mine-ness' means you must physically throw away your home, your family, or your everyday belongings.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means dropping the exhausting mental anxiety of possessiveness, allowing you to care for things lovingly without being destroyed if they change or leave.

verses.philosophy: This is the ultimate state of *Sarvatma-Bhava*—the direct realization that you are the underlying essence of every single form. This realization dissolves the root of egoic security mechanisms, leaving behind a profound, open freedom.

title: Laya Prakaranam
description: The Dissolution of the Objective World
text: In this chapter, the sage Ashtavakra speaks again. Pleased with Janaka's steady integration, he provides four highly concentrated, minimalist instructions to stabilize this realization. This chapter is traditionally called Laya Chatushka (The Four Verses on Dissolution), offering a direct method to dissolve the appearance of the objective world back into the ocean of absolute awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
न ते सङ्गोऽस्ति केनचिद्किं शुद्धस्त्यक्तुमिच्छसि ।
सङ्घातविलयं कुर्वन्नेवमेव लयं व्रज ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
na tē saṅgō'sti kēnacitkiṃ śuddhastyaktumicchasi |
saṅghātavilayaṃ kurvannēvamēva layaṃ vraja ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: You are not attached to anything. Being pure, what do you wish to renounce? Dissolve this artificial complex of the mind-body vehicle, and enter into your natural state of rest.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra cuts right through the spiritual ego's obsession with renunciation. If your true identity is infinite space, it was never actually tangled up with objects to begin with. Trying hard to 'give things up' just keeps the false idea that you were dirty or bound alive.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that becoming highly spiritual requires you to perform extreme acts of physical sacrifice, or throw away your belongings to prove your purity.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that your awareness is already clean and untouched by material things, which frees you from needing to reject them or run away.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Asanga* (ever-unattached). True renunciation is not physical abandonment, but the dissolution (*Laya*) of the false belief that you are identical to the mind-body complex (*Sanghata*).

verses.sanskrit:
उदेति भवतो विश्वं वारिधेरिव बुद्बुदः ।
इति ज्ञात्वैकमात्मानमेवमेव लयं व्रज ॥
verses.transliteration:
udēti bhavatō viśvaṃ vāridhēriva budbudaḥ |
iti jñātvaikamātmānamēvamēva layaṃ vraja ||
verses.english: The universe rises from you like bubbles bursting forth from the ocean. Knowing yourself to be that single, underlying reality, enter into your natural state of rest.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra uses the bubble metaphor to explain existence. A bubble is nothing but a temporary pocket of air wrapped in water. It doesn't have a separate life from the sea. Similarly, the entire universe is just a temporary vibration on the surface of your own awareness.

understand.common_misconception: Feeling small, insignificant, and overwhelmed by a massive, cold universe that exists completely outside of you.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that the entire cosmos is an inside job, arising and passing away directly within your wide-open consciousness.

verses.philosophy: This is *Srishti-Laya Sama*—the realization that creation and dissolution are safe, natural movements of the same source. When you know you are the source, the changing forms lose their power to intimidate you.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रत्यक्षमप्यवस्तुत्वाद् विश्वं नास्त्यमले त्वयि ।
रज्जुसर्प इव व्यक्तमेवमेव लयं व्रज ॥
verses.transliteration:
pratyakṣamapyavastutvād viśvaṃ nāstyamalē tvayi |
rajjusarpa iva vyaktamēvamēva layaṃ vraja ||
verses.english: Even though the universe is visible to your senses, it has no real substance of its own. It does not exist in your pristine, untouched identity, just as the imagined snake does not exist in the rope. Therefore, enter into your natural state of rest.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra handles a common doubt: 'If the world is an illusion, why does it look and feel so solid?' He explains that sensory visibility does not mean absolute reality. A dream looks totally real while you are asleep, but it holds zero physical weight once you wake up.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that calling the world 'unreal' means your eyes will stop processing light or your hands will stop feeling solid objects.
understand.attainment_meaning: You still see and interact with everything normally, but you stop misinterpreting it as an independent, binding trap.

verses.philosophy: This clarifies the concept of *Mithyatva* (falsity). The world has an apparent, functional reality (*Pratibhasika*), but it has no independent, absolute substance (*Paramarthika*) apart from pure awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
समदुःखसुखः पूर्ण आशानैराश्ययोः समः ।
समजीवितमृत्युः सन्नेवमेव लयं व्रज ॥
verses.transliteration:
samaduḥkhasukhaḥ pūrṇa āśānairāśyayōḥ samaḥ |
samajīvitamṛtyuḥ sannēvamēva layaṃ vraja ||
verses.english: Remaining identical through pleasure and pain, complete in yourself, steady in the face of hope or disappointment, and unbothered by life or death, enter into your natural state of rest.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra concludes the chapter with a beautiful description of perfect emotional balance (*Samatva*). When you stop demanding that life provide constant pleasure or validate your ego, you remain steady and content regardless of what the current moment brings.

understand.common_misconception: Fearing that reaching this level of balance will turn you into an unfeeling, robotic stone wall with no room for genuine human experience.
understand.attainment_meaning: It brings a profound, gentle resilience that allows you to experience life's textures deeply without being crushed when they change or pass away.

verses.philosophy: This lands on the state of *Jivanmukti* (living liberation). The mind's deep habits of swinging between desire and aversion are completely dissolved into the spacious, unchanging background of the Self (*Purna*).

title: Prakriti Para
description: The Plane Beyond Natural Duality
text: In this chapter, traditionally known as Prakriti-Para Prakaranam (The Plane Beyond Nature), King Janaka speaks once again. Inspired by Ashtavakra's teaching on dissolution in Chapter 5, Janaka explains his realization using a beautiful, continuous metaphor of infinite space (Akasha). He demonstrates that from the perspective of absolute awareness, all dualities—like action and stillness, mind and world—are completely artificial boundaries that have already dissolved.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
आकाशवदनन्तोऽहं घटवत्प्राकृतं जगत् ।
इति ज्ञानं तथैतस्य न त्यागो न ग्रहो लयः ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
ākāśavadanantō'haṃ ghaṭavatprākrutaṃ jagat |
iti jñānaṃ tathaitasya na tyāgō na grahō layaḥ ||
verses.english: Janaka said: I am infinite like boundless space, and the natural world of form is like a temporary clay pot. This is true knowledge. Therefore, there is nothing to renounce, nothing to hold onto, and nothing to dissolve.
verses.commentary: Janaka masterfully takes Ashtavakra's previous lesson on dissolution to its absolute logical conclusion. If a clay pot is built inside a room, the space inside the pot isn't a different kind of space. When the pot breaks, the space doesn't actually 'dissolve' or go anywhere—it was always just boundless space. Realizing this, Janaka stops trying to manipulate his mind or the world.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must actively destroy your thoughts, silence your mind, or wait for the physical world to disappear to experience true freedom.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that your awareness is already wide-open and boundless like space, completely unaffected by whether thoughts are present or absent.

verses.philosophy: This is the ultimate shift from *Laya* (active dissolution) to *Sahaja* (the ever-present, natural state). The Self is *Akashavat* (space-like). Since the world never truly divided your awareness, tracking spiritual progress or trying to dissolve the world is revealed to be an illusion.

verses.sanskrit:
महोदधिरिवानन्तोऽहं द्वैतवीचिः स्वभावतः ।
उदेति वा शाम्यति वा न मे वृद्धिर्न वा क्षतिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mahōdadhirivānantō'haṃ dvaitavīciḥ svabhāvataḥ |
udēti vā śāmyati vā na mē vṛddhirna vā kṣatiḥ ||
verses.english: I am like the infinite ocean, and the changing scenes of duality are like waves naturally tossing on my surface. Whether they arise or subside, I experience neither growth nor loss.
verses.commentary: Janaka uses a magnificent ocean metaphor. When a massive wave rises, the ocean does not gain an extra gallon of water; when the wave flattens, the ocean does not lose anything. Janaka realizes that his core awareness remains exactly the same, whether his life is chaotic and loud or perfectly quiet.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that a spiritual person's mind must always be perfectly blank, and that having a sudden emotion or a busy day means you have lost your connection.
understand.attainment_meaning: Allowing life’s temporary ups and downs to happen naturally on the surface, while remaining completely anchored in the steady peace underneath.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Avikarya* (unchanging) and *Purna* (eternally full). Relative experiences add zero value to pure consciousness, and difficult experiences take absolutely nothing away from it.

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ विश्वपोतः कल्पनावशात् ।
भ्रमति क्षोभमप्राप्तो न मे वृद्धिश्च न क्षतिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau viśvapōtaḥ kalpanāvaśāt |
bhramati kṣōbhamaprāptō na mē vṛddhiśca na kṣatiḥ ||
verses.english: In the infinite ocean of myself, the boat of the universe wanders here and there, driven by the winds of imagination. Yet I remain completely unbothered, experiencing neither growth nor loss.
verses.commentary: The entire universe is described as a tiny boat drifting on a boundless sea. The boat might encounter wild storms or calm waters, but the deep ocean floor miles below stays entirely silent and still. Janaka observes the grand cosmic drama of life without letting its movements shake his core presence.

understand.common_misconception: Living in constant psychological stress, believing that you are personally responsible for managing and micro-managing every external event around you.
understand.attainment_meaning: Stepping back into the vast space of awareness, allowing the world to unfold naturally without letting it disrupt your internal balance.

verses.philosophy: The universe possesses an imagined status (*Kalpanamatra*). Because the witness does not participate in the movement of the objects it observes, it remains completely free from worldly friction (*Kshobha*).

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ विश्वं नाम तरङ्गिका ।
उदेति वा शाम्यति वा न मे वृद्धिर्न वा क्षतिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
