mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau viśvaṃ nāma taraṅgikā |
udēti vā śāmyati vā na mē vṛddhirna vā kṣatiḥ ||
verses.english: In the infinite ocean of myself, the thing called the universe is nothing but a tiny, transient ripple. Whether it rises or falls, I experience neither growth nor loss.
verses.commentary: Janaka closes the chapter by shrinking the massive universe down to a tiny, insignificant ripple (*taraṅgikā*) on his vast ocean of awareness. By changing the scale of his identity from a fragile human body to infinite space, the weight of all his problems, worries, and fears disappears in an instant.

understand.common_misconception: Treating your personal identity and individual problems as the absolute center of reality.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that your personal life is just a beautiful, brief moment inside a limitless field of consciousness, which instantly takes the pressure off.

verses.philosophy: This lands on the absolute standpoint of Advaita Vedanta: *Paramartha Drishti*. The entire space-time universe is just a temporary ripple on the surface of your true identity, leaving your absolute presence completely untouched, stainless, and ever-free.

title: Tattva Jnana
description: The Stability of Absolute Realization
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Tattva Jnana Prakaranam (The Plane of Absolute Realization), King Janaka continues to speak. He refines his ocean metaphor even further to describe the exact nature of a Jivanmukta (one who is liberated while living). He explains that when the illusion of a separate personal ego completely dissolves, the mind rests in a state of unshakeable quiet, looking at the entire world without a single trace of attachment or aversion.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ विश्वपोते कल्पनावशात् ।
यत्र तत्र चरत्वेव न मे वृद्धिश्च न क्षतिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau viśvapōtē kalpanāvaśāt |
yatra tatra caratvēva na mē vṛddhiśca na kṣatiḥ ||
verses.english: Janaka said: In the infinite ocean of myself, let the boat of the universe wander wherever it wants, driven by its own momentum. I experience neither growth nor loss.
verses.commentary: Janaka looks out at the natural movements of life and drops all desire to control them. He realizes that trying to steer, stop, or fix the outer world implies that his inner peace is dependent on external conditions. He allows the universe to float freely within his awareness.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that spiritual awakening will magically grant you supernatural power to control every external event and person around you.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing you no longer need to control external events because your inner state is completely independent of them.

verses.philosophy: This is the integration of *Udacinata* (spiritual indifference/neutrality). The world moves according to its own natural laws (*Prarabdha*), while the witness remains perfectly steady and unbothered.

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ जगच्चित्रं विलोक्यताम् ।
अहो न मे वृद्धिर्न क्षतिः परमात्मास्वरूपतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau jagaccitraṃ vilōkyatāmd |
ahō na mē vṛddhirna kṣatiḥ paramātmāsvārupataḥ ||
verses.english: In the infinite ocean of myself, the colorful painting of the world appears. Wonderful am I! I experience neither growth nor loss, remaining ever fixed in my supreme nature.
verses.commentary: Janaka compares the universe to a vast canvas or painting (*jagaccitraṃ*). A painting contains diverse scenes—storms, battles, or quiet landscapes—but the canvas underneath never gets wet, burned, or torn by the images painted on it. Janaka views life as a beautiful, harmless display.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must close your eyes and block out all sensory beauty, art, and human experiences to be truly spiritual.
understand.attainment_meaning: Watching and enjoying the world's diverse textures fully, while knowing that none of them can stain or alter who you really are.

verses.philosophy: The world has an *Adhyasika* (superimposed) relationship with the Self. Consciousness plays the role of the ultimate screen, completely hosting the cosmic movie without absorbing any of its damage.

verses.sanskrit:
मय्यनन्तमहाम्भोधौ न प्रपञ्चो न कल्पितम् ।
अगाधोऽहं निरातङ्को बोधमात्रोऽहममलः ॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyanantamahāmbhōdhau na prapañcō na kalpitam |
agādhō'haṃ nirātaṅkō bōdhamātrō'hamamalaḥ ||
verses.english: In the infinite ocean of myself, there is actually no objective world and no mental projection. I am fathomless, free from anxiety, pure awareness, and completely stainless.
verses.commentary: Janaka shifts from the relative viewpoint to the absolute standpoint. When you dive deep into the ocean, the waves on the surface disappear from view. Similarly, when Janaka rests deeply in pure awareness, the mind's anxious projections lose their grip entirely.

understand.common_misconception: Living in constant spiritual anxiety, continuously trying to fight off bad thoughts or force your mind to be quiet.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that your core awareness is already quiet, deep, and completely safe, far below the surface noise of the mind.

verses.philosophy: This is *Nirvikalpa Sthiti*—the unconditioned state. From the absolute perspective (*Paramartha*), the world doesn't even exist as a separate trap to be escaped from; there is only pure, boundless awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
अहो मयि चिन्मात्रे न विशेषाः पृथक् पृथक् ।
क्व बन्धः क्व च वा मोक्षः कल्पनाविरतिः मम ॥
verses.transliteration:
ahō mayi cinmātrē na viśēṣāḥ pṛthak pṛthak |
kva bandhaḥ kva ca vā mōkṣaḥ kalpanāviratiḥ mama ||
verses.english: Oh, in me, the pure consciousness, no separate divisions exist! Where then is bondage, and where is liberation? My mind's habit of projecting stories has completely ceased.
verses.commentary: Janaka points out that concepts like 'trapped' or 'free' are just mental labels. A wave on the ocean cannot be 'bound' by the water around it. When the mind stops creating these binary labels, the artificial struggle ends.

understand.common_misconception: Treating liberation as a future spiritual prize that you must struggle and fight to attain.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that the entire struggle was created by the mind, and dropping that story brings immediate peace.

verses.philosophy: Bondage (*Bandha*) and Liberation (*Moksha*) are relative terms that belong entirely to the domain of the ego (*Ahamkara*). Pure consciousness (*Cinmatra*) is completely undivided and beyond both categories.

verses.sanskrit:
शरीरं स्वर्गनरकौ बन्धमोक्षौ भयं तथा ।
कल्पनामात्रमेवैतत् किं मे कार्यं चिदात्मनः ॥
verses.transliteration:
śarīraṃ svarganarakau bandhamokṣau bhayaṃ tathā |
kalpanāmātramevaitat kiṃ me kāryaṃ cidātmanaḥ ||
verses.english: The body, heaven and hell, bondage and liberation, as well as fear—these are all mere projections of the mind. What have I to do with any of them, I who am pure consciousness?
verses.commentary: Janaka concludes his ecstatic declaration by sweeping away all dualistic frameworks. Social rankings, religious fears of hell, and spiritual desires for heaven are recognized as mental graphics. Anchored as pure consciousness, Janaka drops all psychological baggage and rests in perfect, natural freedom.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that true peace requires you to conform to rigid religious dogmas, rules, and superstitious tracking systems.
understand.attainment_meaning: Stepping out of all mental traps entirely, knowing that your true nature is eternally free from both worldly and heavenly anxieties.

verses.philosophy: This lands on *Jivanmukti* in its purest form. The mind's deep habits of swinging between fear and craving are completely dissolved into the spacious, unchanging background of the Self.

title: Asanga Prakaranam
description: The Mechanics of Bondage and Freedom
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Asanga Prakaranam (The Plane of True Non-Attachment), the sage Ashtavakra speaks again. He outlines the exact definition of psychological bondage and ultimate freedom. He explains that bondage doesn't depend on what objects are around you, but rather on whether your mind is caught up in the heavy habits of grasping, craving, or rejecting.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
तदा बन्धो यदा चित्तं किञ्चिद् वाञ्छति शोचति ।
किञ्चिन् मुञ्चति गृह्णाति किञ्चिद् धृष्यति कुप्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
tadā bandhō yadā cittaṃ kiñcid vāñchati śōcati |
kiñcin muñcati gṛhṇāti kiñcid dhṛṣyati kupyati ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: It is bondage when the mind desires or grieves over anything, rejects or holds onto anything, or feels happy or angry about anything.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra provides a baseline psychological definition of bondage. Bondage is not a physical rope; it is the mind's reactive movement. The moment the mind reacts with intense clinging or aggressive aversion to passing events, it creates the illusion of being trapped.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that you are only bound if you live a highly materialistic life or possess deep physical vices.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that even subtle mental reactions—like obsessing over spiritual progress or getting angry at bad weather—keep the loop of bondage alive.

verses.philosophy: Bondage is an internal, operational condition (*Citta-Vritti*). It occurs when consciousness identifies with emotional swings like attraction (*Raga*) and repulsion (*Dvesha*).

verses.sanskrit:
तदा मुक्तिर्यदा चित्तं न वाञ्छति न शोचति ।
न मुञ्चति न गृह्णाति न हृष्यति न कुप्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
tadā muktiryadā cittaṃ na vāñchati na śōcati |
na muñcati na gṛhṇāti na hṛṣyati na kupyati ||
verses.english: It is liberation when the mind neither desires nor grieves over anything, neither rejects nor holds onto anything, and neither celebrates nor gets angry about anything.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra gives the exact inverse formula for liberation. True freedom is the cooling down of the mind's reactive engine. When the mind can host experiences smoothly without being thrown off balance by them, it rests in its natural, liberated state.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that a liberated mind must become blank, dead, or completely incapable of experiencing any human feelings.
understand.attainment_meaning: Thoughts and events still occur normally, but the mind stops sticking to them, allowing emotions to pass through like writing on water.

verses.philosophy: This is *Samatvam* (perfect equanimity). Liberation is not a spatial relocation to a heavenly realm; it is the peaceful centering of awareness right here and now.

verses.sanskrit:
तदा बन्धो यदा चित्तं सक्तं कास्वपि दृष्टिषु ।
तदा मुक्तिर्यदा चित्तं असक्तं सर्वदृष्टिषु ॥
verses.transliteration:
tadā bन्धो yadā cittaṃ saktaṃ kāsvapi dṛṣṭiṣu |
tadā muktiryadā cittaṃ asaktaṃ sarvadṛṣṭiṣu ||
verses.english: It is bondage when the mind gets heavily attached to any particular viewpoint or ideology. It is liberation when the mind is completely detached from all viewpoints.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra targets intellectual attachment. Getting dogmatic about philosophies, spiritual rules, or lifestyle systems keeps the mind rigid and separate. A truly free soul uses concepts as temporary tools, but remains detached from all rigid mental positions.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that spiritual awakening means picking the single 'correct' intellectual theory and aggressively defending it against everyone else.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the need to be intellectually right, stepping back into the vast space of awareness that holds all perspectives.

verses.philosophy: This addresses *Drishti-Asanga*—non-attachment to theories. Pure consciousness is beyond all conceptual models (*Vikalpa*); defending an ideology merely reconstructs the personal ego.

verses.sanskrit:
यदा नाहं तदा मोक्षो यदाहं बन्धनं तथा ।
मत्वेति हेलया किञ्चिन्मा गृहाण विमुञ्च मा ॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā nāhaṃ tadā mōkṣō yadāhaṃ bandanaṃ tathā |
matvēti hēlayā kiñcinmā gṛhāṇa vimuñca mā ||
verses.english: When the separate 'I' ego is absent, there is absolute liberation; when that 'I' is present, there is bondage. Realizing this clearly, gracefully live your life without grabbing onto or pushing away anything.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra delivers the ultimate summary of the chapter. The root of all trouble is the word 'me'—the separate personal claim over experiences. When this false center dissolves, you can live life with effortless grace (*helaya*), meeting each moment exactly as it is.

understand.common_misconception: Trying to forcefully kill your ego through intense spiritual practices or continuous self-punishment.
understand.attainment_meaning: Simply seeing through the ego, recognizing it is just a collection of thoughts, which causes its heavy grip to slip away naturally.

verses.philosophy: This lands on the core Advaita principle of *Ahamkara-Nivritti* (the dissolution of the ego-sense). When the false separation between the seeker and reality dissolves, what remains is the boundless, unattached witness.

title: Chitta Nirveda
description: Detachment from Worldly Debates and Duties
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Chitta-Nirveda Prakaranam (The Plane of Indifference to Worldly Debates), the sage Ashtavakra explains how to deal with the endless contradictions of worldly opinions, duties, and social rules. He reveals that trying to resolve every debate or fulfill every expectation is an endless trap. True peace comes from simply observing these shifting rules with detached amusement, letting them go to rest in the quiet center of the Self.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
कृताकृते च द्वन्द्वानि कदा शान्तानि कस्य वा ।
एवं ज्ञात्वेह निर्वेदाद्भव त्यागी व्रती विना ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
kṛtākṛte ca dvandvāni kadā śāntāni kasya vā |
evaṃ jñātveha nirvedādbhava tyāgī vratī vinā ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: When or for whom have the dualities of duties performed and duties left unperformed ever ceased? Knowing this completely, become indifferent to these rules and drop your rigid vows without any pride.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra highlights the exhausting nature of societal and religious obligations. There will always be one more thing to do, one more expectation to meet, or another rule to follow. Trying to satisfy all of them to find peace is impossible. True peace arrives only when you step off the treadmill entirely.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you can only become spiritually free after you have perfectly settled every single worldly problem or satisfied everyone's expectations.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that worldly demands are endless, and choosing to drop your internal attachment to them right now.

verses.philosophy: This addresses the limitations of *Karma-Kanda* (the path of ritualistic duties). Rituals and duties are relative; they belong to the world of action and cannot produce the timeless state of pure awareness.

verses.sanskrit:
कस्य वा तात कस्यापि भाग्यवतोऽवलोकनात् ।
जगतश्चेष्टा स्वभावोऽयं संशान्ता धीरचेतसः ॥
verses.transliteration:
kasya vā tāta kasyāpi bhāgyavato'valokanāt |
jagataśceṣṭā svabhāvo'yaṃ saṃśāntā dhīracetasaḥ ||
verses.english: My dear child, rare is that fortunate person whose mind goes completely quiet simply by observing the natural, frantic movements of the world.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra honors the rare individual who wakes up simply by looking at the rat race around them. By observing how people run in circles chasing money, praise, and comfort—only to lose them all anyway—the wise person instantly loses interest in the illusion.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that spiritual awakening requires complex, secret training that takes decades to understand.
understand.attainment_meaning: It can happen in a single moment of clear observation, simply by seeing the utter pointlessness of ego-driven chasing.

verses.philosophy: This is *Nirveda*—a profound, clear-eyed indifference born from observing the temporary nature of the world (*Samsara*). It serves as a powerful catalyst for returning to the unmoving background.

verses.sanskrit:
सर्वमनित्यमेवेदं तापत्रयविदूषितम् ।
असारं निन्दितं हेयमिति ज्ञात्वा सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvamanityamevedaṃ tāpatrayavidūṣitam |
asāraṃ ninditaṃ heyamiti jñātvā sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: Everything in this world is temporary, stained by the three kinds of suffering, without real substance, and bound to decay. Knowing this for certain, drop your anxiety and be happy.
verses.commentary: The 'three sufferings' (*tapatraya*) refer to troubles caused by our own minds and bodies, troubles caused by other people, and troubles caused by natural events. Ashtavakra tells us to accept that the world is inherently unstable, which stops us from expecting it to be perfect.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that calling the world 'substanceless' is a depressing, cynical perspective that ruins your joy in life.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is incredibly liberating because you stop putting the unfair pressure on temporary things to give you permanent happiness.

verses.philosophy: This aligns with the essential *Anitya* (impermanence) doctrine. Seeing that everything in the objective world changes prevents the mind from seeking refuge in passing phenomena.

verses.sanskrit:
को वा कालो वयः किं वा यत्र द्वन्द्वानि नो नृणाम् ।
तान्युपेक्ष्य यथाप्राप्तवर्ती सिद्धिमवाप्नुयात् ॥
verses.transliteration:
ko vā kālo vayaḥ kiṃ vā yatra dvandvāni no nṛṇām |
tānupekṣya yathāprāptavartī siddhimavāpnuyāt ||
verses.english: Is there any age or time when human beings are completely free from dualities? Ignoring these shifting conditions, live gracefully with whatever naturally arrives, and reach fulfillment.
verses.commentary: Life will always present dualities: youth versus old age, health versus sickness, praise versus criticism. Ashtavakra points out that waiting for the perfect condition to arrive before being happy is a fool's game. True freedom is living openly with whatever is happening right now.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that an enlightened life is a magical, static state where you never experience a rainy day, physical aging, or difficult moments.
understand.attainment_meaning: Allowing conditions to change naturally on the outside, while your inner awareness remains totally unshakeable and content.

verses.philosophy: This lands on *Yathaprapta-Vartana*—spontaneously flowing with whatever life brings without resistance. It aligns your daily actions with the absolute, unattached witness.

verses.sanskrit:
महर्षीणां योगिनां च कस्य वा मतमुत्तमम् ।
ज्ञात्वैवं निर्वेदं गतः कश्चिन्न शाम्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
maharṣīṇāṃ yogināṃ ca kasya vā matamuttamam |
jñātvaivaṃ nirvedaṃ gataḥ kaścinna śāmyati ||
verses.english: Which great sage or yogi has ever agreed on the single 'best' philosophy? Seeing the endless contradictions in opinions, become indifferent to the debates, and discover complete peace.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra exposes the trap of spiritual intellectualism. Even the greatest thinkers and teachers argue over definitions, paths, and techniques. If you try to find peace by picking the 'perfect' opinion, your mind will stay busy forever. True peace is found by stepping back from the arguments entirely.

understand.common_misconception: Spending years aggressively debating spiritual theories, believing that winning the argument means you are closer to truth.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that truth is found in the quiet space *before* thoughts and arguments take shape.

verses.philosophy: This represents *Vada-Upeksha*—the transcendence of philosophical debates. Pure consciousness cannot be captured by language or mental concepts, making all absolute claims on truth relative overlays.

verses.sanskrit:
कृत्वा चैतन्यनिर्वैदात् कश्चिद् वै न विचारतः ।
यः साक्षात् कुरुते धीरः सोऽहं भावं विमुच्य वै ॥
verses.transliteration:
kṛtvā caitanyanirvaidāt kaścid vai na vicārataḥ |
yaḥ sākṣāt kurute dhīraḥ so'haṃ bhāvaṃ vimucya vai ||
verses.english: The wise leader is not the one who merely reasons intellectually, but the one who through complete disillusionment with the world directly perceives their true identity, dropping the false 'I' ego.
verses.commentary: Intellectual study (*vicara*) can only take you so far. It can point you in the right direction, but it cannot make you free. Ashtavakra states that real transformation happens only when you are completely disillusioned with the ego's games, allowing you to experience reality directly.

understand.common_misconception: Confusing a massive collection of spiritual knowledge with actual personal transformation and presence.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the conceptual baggage and resting simply as the quiet space of awareness right now.

verses.philosophy: This emphasizes *Aparoksha Anubhuti*—direct, unmediated experience. True realization is not an intellectual deduction; it is the immediate collapse of the separate seeker into reality itself.

verses.sanskrit:
भावानां स्फुरणं विद्धि चिन्मात्रं न पृथक् ततः ।
अतोऽहमिति मत्वैव बन्धमोक्षौ न चिन्तयेत् ॥
verses.transliteration:
bhāvānāṃ sphuraṇaṃ viddhi cinmātraṃ na pṛthak tataḥ |
ato'hamiti matvaiva bandhamokṣau na cintayet ||
verses.english: Recognize that all changing forms are nothing but variations of pure consciousness; they are not separate from it. Knowing this, stop obsessing over thoughts of bondage or liberation.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra simplifies the entire cosmic landscape. Everything you see, hear, or think is just consciousness taking a temporary shape. When you know you are that consciousness, worrying about whether you are 'bound' or 'free' is revealed to be a pointless mental story.

understand.common_misconception: Treating your day-to-day mind as a dangerous enemy that you must constantly fight, conquer, and destroy.
understand.attainment_meaning: Seeing that your mind is just a natural wave on your ocean of awareness, which removes its power to bother you.

verses.philosophy: This is the absolute core of Advaita Vedanta: *Sarvam Khalvidam Brahma* (Everything is Brahman). When the appearance of a separate world dissolves into the reality of pure consciousness, all relative concepts like bondage and freedom disappear.

verses.sanskrit:
उपेक्षां भावनां कृत्वा द्वन्द्वेषु च स्वतः स्थिते ।
सर्वभूतगते शान्ते न किञ्चिद् अवशिष्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
upekṣāṃ bhāvanāṃ kṛtvā dvandveṣu ca svataḥ sthite |
sarvabhūtagate śānte na kiñcid avaśiṣyate ||
verses.english: Remaining perfectly indifferent to dualities, settled in your own natural state, and resting in that peace which fills all beings—nothing else remains to be done.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra closes the chapter by pointing to absolute rest. When you stop chasing conditions, stop fighting with your thoughts, and stop validating the ego's worries, your awareness naturally settles down. You realize you are already home, and the search is beautifully over.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that achieving spiritual rest will turn you into an idle, useless person who can no longer enjoy a productive life.
understand.attainment_meaning: It allows your actions to flow from a place of deep joy and presence, entirely free from the frantic stress of trying to prove yourself.

verses.philosophy: This is the state of *Krita-Krityata*—the ultimate completion of all actions. Awareness rests in its own continuous, all-pervading nature, leaving behind no separate ego, no anxiety, and no further steps to take.

title: Vairagya Prakaranam
description: The Dissolution of Desire and Worldly Chasing
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Vairagya Prakaranam (The Plane of Absolute Dispassion), the sage Ashtavakra delivers a direct, uncompromising strike against the root drivers of the psychological ego: the pursuit of pleasure, the accumulation of wealth, and the subtle desire for spiritual or social merit. He reveals that these pursuits are endless loops that keep the mind fractured, and that true serenity is realized only when you step out of the desiring mechanism altogether.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
विहाय वैरिणं काममर्थं चानर्थसङ्कुलम् ।
धर्ममप्येतयोरिष्टं सर्वत्रानादरं कुरु ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
vihāya vairiṇaṃ kāmamarthaṃ cānarthasaṅkulam |
dharmamapyētayōriṣṭaṃ sarvatrānādaraṃ kuru ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: Abandon desire, which is the ultimate enemy; abandon wealth, which is a breeding ground for endless anxieties and troubles; and abandon institutional merit, which relies on both. Become completely indifferent to them all.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra targets the traditional pillars of worldly life (*Trivarga*: Kama, Artha, Dharma). He cuts through the illusion that collecting more assets or achieving higher social standing can bring absolute peace, exposing them as sophisticated traps that keep the mind in a perpetual state of lack.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must force yourself into a state of physical poverty or aggressively hate money and comfort to be pure.
understand.attainment_meaning: Breaking your emotional and psychological dependence on external assets for validation, realizing your baseline value is already absolute.

verses.philosophy: Worldly ambition feeds on the illusion of incompletion (*Apurnata*). Dispassion (*Vairagya*) is not an emotional reaction of hatred toward the world, but a clear-eyed recognition of its temporary nature.

verses.sanskrit:
मित्रक्षेत्रधनागारदारादायादिसम्पदः ।
पश्येह मायया बद्धान् स्वप्नेन्द्रजालवत् क्षणात् ॥
verses.transliteration:
mitrakṣētradhanāgāradārādāyādisampadaḥ |
paśyēha māyayā baddhān svapnēndrajālavat kṣaṇāt ||
verses.english: Look upon friends, properties, wealth, houses, family, and all fortunes as a passing dream or a magician's brief illusion, lasting only for a moment.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra invites us to take a panoramic look at time. The relationships, buildings, and fortunes we fight so hard to secure are temporary arrangements. They shift and disappear over time, much like the changing graphics of a nightly dream.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that calling relationships 'dream-like' means you should stop loving your family or become a cold, uncaring person.
understand.attainment_meaning: It allows you to appreciate and care for the people in your life deeply right now, without suffocating them with anxious possessiveness.

verses.philosophy: This is the evaluation of things as *Mithya* (dependently real). Objects have a brief, functional reality (*Vyavaharika*), but they lack permanent substance, making any egoic attachment to them a guarantee of future grief.

verses.sanskrit:
यत्र यत्र भवेत्तृष्णा संसारं विद्धि तत्र वै ।
प्रौढवैराग्यमाश्रित्य वीततृष्णः सुखी भव ॥
verses.transliteration:
yatra yatra bhavēttṛṣṇā saṃsāraṃ viddhi tatra vai |
prauḍhavairāgyamāśritya vītatṛṣṇaḥ sukhī bhava ||
verses.english: Wherever a trace of craving or mental thirst exists, recognize that very place as the realm of bondage. Resting in mature dispassion, free yourself from that thirst and be happy.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra identifies the mechanism of bondage: *Trishna* (craving/thirst). Bondage is not caused by the physical objects around you; it is created by the sticky mental projection that says, 'If I lose this object, I lose my happiness.'

understand.common_misconception: Blaming your environment, your job, or your city for your stress, assuming that moving to a new location will automatically fix your mind.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that stress travels inside your own mind as craving, and dropping that internal habit brings immediate freedom wherever you stand.

verses.philosophy: Bondage is completely non-spatial. It is an internal state of fixation (*Sanga*). Mature dispassion (*Praudha Vairagya*) dissolves the false value assigned to objects, restoring consciousness to its natural autonomy.

verses.sanskrit:
तृष्णामात्रात्मको बन्धस्तन्नाशो मोक्ष उच्यते ।
भवा संसृक्तिविरत्या केवलं तुष्टिमात्रतः ॥
verses.transliteration:
tṛṣṇāmātrātmakō bandhastannāśō mōkṣa ucyatē |
bhavā saṃsṛktiviratyā kēvalaṃ tuṣṭimātrataḥ ||
verses.english: Bondage consists entirely of craving, and its destruction is what is called liberation. Simply by stepping back from worldly chasing, find continuous satisfaction in your own nature.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra simplifies the entire spiritual path into a binary formula: craving is bondage; the absence of craving is freedom. You do not need to construct a specialized spiritual identity; you simply need to stop running after external patches to fix an imagined inner lack.

understand.common_misconception: Treating liberation as a difficult, future prize that requires you to collect endless spiritual experiences and certifications.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is a process of subtraction, not addition. It is the peace that remains when your desperate need for the next distraction drops away.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Sva-Tripta*—intrinsically satisfied in itself. Craving is a temporary misunderstanding that assumes satisfaction must be imported from external objects.

verses.sanskrit:
त्वमेकश्चेतनः शुद्धो जडं विश्वमसत्तथा ।
अविद्यापि न काचित्स्वा का बुभुत्सा तथापि ते ॥
verses.transliteration:
tvamēkaścetanaḥ śuddhō jaḍaṃ viśvamasattathā |
avidyāpi na kācitsvā kā bubhutsā tathāpi tē ||
verses.english: You are the solitary, pure, conscious awareness. The material universe is insentient and temporary. Even ignorance itself is completely non-existent. What else is left for you to anxiously seek or know?
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra removes even the intellectual anxiety of trying to figure out 'why' ignorance exists. He points out that the material world is just a passing scene, and you are the conscious space hosting it. Worrying about analyzing the illusion is just another trick to keep your mind busy.

understand.common_misconception: Spending years obsessing over spiritual theories, trying to build a perfect mental map of cosmic dimensions and energies.
understand.attainment_meaning: Stepping out of the intellectual tracking loop altogether, resting simply as the clear presence that observes all concepts.

verses.philosophy: This touches on *Anirvachaniya*—the ultimate indefinability of ignorance. From the highest standpoint (*Paramartha*), ignorance has no real independent footing; looking for it is like looking for darkness with a flashlight.

verses.sanskrit:
राज्यं सुताः कलत्राणि शरीराणि सुखानि च ।
संसक्तस्यापि नष्टानि तव जन्मातिजन्मनि ॥
verses.transliteration:
rājyaṃ sutāḥ kalatrāṇi śarīrāṇi sukhāni ca |
saṃsaktasyāpi naṣṭāni tava janmātijanmani ||
verses.english: Kingdoms, children, relationships, physical bodies, and luxuries have all slipped through your fingers across countless lifetimes, despite your deep attachment to them. They are gone.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra contextually reminds Janaka of his historical shifts. Even if you manage to secure perfect worldly conditions, health, and praise today, the river of time will eventually wash them away. Realizing that you have played this game across vast stretches of time helps break the panic of the current moment.

understand.common_misconception: Treating your current set of problems, status, and worldly dramas as the absolute, permanent center of the entire universe.
understand.attainment_meaning: Gaining a wide perspective on your life, which shrinks your current anxieties down into minor, passing ripples.

verses.philosophy: The evolutionary cycle of the ego (*Samsara*) is an endless loop of acquisition and loss. Recognizing this inevitable pattern triggers a natural shift in attention away from the passing scene and back to the permanent viewer.

verses.sanskrit:
अलमर्थेन कामेन सुकृतेनापि कर्मणा ।
एभ्यः संसारकान्तारे न विश्रामोऽभवन्मनः ॥
verses.transliteration:
alamarthēna kāmēna sukrutēnāpi karmaṇā |
ēbhyaḥ saṃsārakāntārē na viśrāmō'bhavanmanaḥ ||
verses.english: Enough with wealth, enough with pleasure, and enough with good deeds! The mind has never found permanent rest or safety in this wild jungle of worldly chasing.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra calls for an absolute halt: 'Enough!' (*Alam*). He explicitly groups 'good moral deeds' along with wealth and pleasure. Why? Because if you perform good deeds simply to feel proud, earn spiritual points, or secure a spot in a pleasant afterlife, you are still operating from a sense of lack.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that spiritual peace is a safe harbor you can build by checking off a list of perfect behaviors or collecting good deeds.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the exhaustive spiritual tracking systems and resting directly in the quiet presence that is already available right now.

verses.philosophy: This is the transition from *Sakam Karma* (desire-driven action) to absolute quietude. The worldly mind is a forest of concepts; true rest (*Visrama*) is found only when the ego stops trying to negotiate with reality.

verses.sanskrit:
कृतं न कति जन्मानि कायेन मनसा गिरा ।
दुःखमायासदं तस्माद्विरमद्वैव पावन ॥
verses.transliteration:
krutaṃ na kati janmāni kāyēna manasā girā |
duḥkhamāyāsadaṃ tasmādviramadvaiva pāvana ||
verses.english: For how many lifetimes have you labored tirelessly with your body, mind, and speech, only to arrive at recurring fatigue and stress? Therefore, O pure one, stop now.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra closes the chapter with a gentle but firm command: 'Stop now' (*virama*). He highlights the profound exhaustion of the ego's continuous efforts to fix, improve, and defend itself. He reminds Janaka of his inherent purity (*pāvana*), inviting him to drop the struggle and rest in his natural state.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that discovering inner peace requires a massive, complex, and strenuous effort that you must continuously maintain.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that peace is the effortless state that remains when you simply stop running away from the present moment.

verses.philosophy: This lands on *Nivritti*—the total cessation of egoic striving. Actions continue to flow naturally through the physical container as needed, but the internal, frantic driver stops completely, dissolving into the boundless, peaceful ocean of pure awareness.

title: Drishti Prakaranam
description: Wisdom and the Natural Unfolding of Reality
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Drishti Prakaranam (The Plane of Wisdom and Divine Order), the sage Ashtavakra describes the mindset of a person who has completely integrated non-dual awareness. He explains that true peace comes from recognizing Prarabdha—the natural, spontaneous unfolding of cosmic intelligence. When you realize that the universe moves by its own laws, the mind stops fighting reality, dropping all trace of anxiety, calculation, and stress.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
भाव अभाव विकारश्च स्वभाव इति निश्चयी ।
निर्विकारो गतक्लेशः सुखेनेवोपशाम्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
bhāva abhāva vikāraśca svabhāva iti niścayī |
nirvikārō gataklēśaḥ sukhēnēvōpaśāmyati ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: One who knows for certain that existence, non-existence, and all change are simply the natural properties of cosmic order, becomes unchanging and free from distress, easily settling into peace.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra outlines the baseline stance of a sage. Things appear, things morph, and things vanish—this is just the natural rhythm of material reality. When you stop taking these cosmic seasons personally, the mind stops reacting with panic to every change.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that spiritual awakening will freeze your life into a static, perfect condition where things never break, decay, or leave.
understand.attainment_meaning: Allowing the natural cycles of growth and decay to happen around you normally, while your inner awareness remains steady.

verses.philosophy: This is the realization of *Svabhava* (universal nature). Change belongs exclusively to the objective world (*Prakriti*). Realizing that the witness (*Atman*) is distinct from these changes dissolves all psychological distress (*Klesha*).

verses.sanskrit:
ईश्वरः सर्वनिर्माता नेह कश्चिद्यत इति निश्चयी ।
अन्तर्गळितसर्वाशः शान्तः क्वापि न सज्जते ॥
verses.transliteration:
īśvaraḥ sarvanirmātā nēha kaścidyata iti niścayī |
antargāḷitasarvāśaḥ śāntaḥ kvāpi na sajjatē ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain that the divine cosmic intelligence is the sole creator and coordinator of everything, finds all internal anxieties dissolved. Perfectly serene, they no longer cling to anything.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra uses the term *Ishvara* to represent the total, interconnected laws of the cosmos. If a supreme cosmic intelligence is running the universe—managing the stars, the oceans, and your heartbeat—the personal ego can finally drop its exhausting habit of trying to micro-manage life.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that surrendering to cosmic intelligence means becoming lazy, fatalistic, or irresponsible in your daily work.
understand.attainment_meaning: It means doing your part completely, but letting go of the frantic, controlling anxiety over the final outcome.

verses.philosophy: This bridges absolute non-duality with the functional understanding of cosmic law. When the ego surrenders its claim of personal authorship, its desires (*Asa*) melt away, leaving behind a deep, open presence.

verses.sanskrit:
आपदः सम्पदः काले दैवादेवेति निश्चयी ।
तृप्तः स्वस्थेन्द्रियो नित्यं न वाञ्छति न शोचति ॥
verses.transliteration:
āpadaḥ sampadaḥ kālē daivādēvēti niścayī |
tṛptaḥ svasthēndriyō nityaṃ na vaāñchati na śōcati ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain that adversity and prosperity arrive in their own time due to cosmic destiny, remains completely satisfied and self-contained. They neither crave nor grieve.
verses.commentary: Life brings alternating waves: sometimes financial or emotional fortune peaks, sometimes unexpected hardships hit. Ashtavakra explains that both states are temporary seasons orchestrated by time. A wise person hosts both seasons without letting them dictate their baseline value.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that financial difficulties or physical illnesses are absolute proof that you have failed spiritually.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that external ups and downs are just part of life's passing script, while your inner contentment remains untouched.

verses.philosophy: This addresses *Prarabdha Karma*—the momentum of past conditions playing out in the present. Because the sage treats external events as natural occurrences (*Daiva*), they remain centered in their own nature (*Svastha*).

verses.sanskrit:
सुखदुःखे जन्ममृत्यू दैवादेवेति निश्चयी ।
साध्यदर्शी निरायासः कुर्वन्नपि न लिप्यते ॥
verses.transliteration:
sukhaduḥkhē janmamṛtyū daivādēvēti niścayī |
sādhyadarśī nirāyāsaḥ kurvannapi na lipyatē ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain that pleasure and pain, birth and death, are driven by cosmic law, looks beyond immediate appearances. Living without strain, they stay clean even while performing actions.
verses.commentary: Birth and death are the primary biological brackets of the human form. Ashtavakra points out that they are natural events under cosmic jurisdiction. By looking beyond the brief flash of human life to the timeless awareness underneath, the fear of mortality disappears completely.

understand.common_misconception: Living in continuous psychological terror of aging, sickness, and physical death.
understand.attainment_meaning: Caring for your body naturally, while knowing that your core consciousness was never born and can never be deleted.

verses.philosophy: The sage is *Nirayasa* (strainless/effortless). They allow actions to move through their physical body to fulfill worldly duties, but because they have dropped the egoic claim of ownership, no new psychological karma clings to them.

verses.sanskrit:
चिन्तया जायते दुःखं नान्यथेह इति निश्चयी ।
तया हीनः सुखी शान्तः सर्वत्र गळितस्पृहः ॥
verses.transliteration:
cintayā jāyatē duḥkhaṃ nānyathēha iti niścayī |
tayā hīnaḥ sukhī śāntaḥ sarvatra gāḷitaspṛhaḥ ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain that suffering arises solely from mental worry and nothing else, becomes free from anxiety. Joyful and calm, their cravings dissolve in every situation.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra delivers a direct insight into the root of human suffering. External events themselves do not possess the power to make you suffer; suffering is manufactured exclusively by the mind's anxious resistance, overthinking, and continuous projection of worst-case scenarios.

understand.common_misconception: Blaming your external situations, your past history, or other people for causing your inner misery and emotional exhaustion.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that while you cannot control every situation, you can stop the internal overthinking loop that turns situations into deep suffering.

verses.philosophy: Suffering (*Duhkha*) is an analytical error generated by *Chinta* (mental worry/conceptual spinning). Dropping this cognitive distortion returns awareness directly to its natural, undisturbed state.

verses.sanskrit:
नाहं देहो न मे देहो बोधोऽहमिति निश्चयी ।
कैवल्यं इव सम्प्राप्तो न स्मरत्युक्तमकृतम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
nāhaṃ dēhō na mē dēhō bōdhō'hamiti niścayī |
kaivalyaṃ iva samprāptō na smaratyuktamakṛtam ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain, 'I am not this body, nor does this body define me; I am pure awareness,' rests in absolute freedom. They stop obsessing over what they did or left undone.
verses.commentary: The wise person stops keeping a mental scorecard of their past actions. Guilt, regrets about old mistakes, and anxiety about unfulfilled spiritual duties belong to the ego's history. Anchored as pure awareness, the sage lives completely in the present moment, free from the weight of memory.

understand.common_misconception: Carrying a lifelong weight of guilt, shame, and self-criticism over past mistakes and bad choices.
understand.attainment_meaning: Learning the practical lesson from a mistake, and then letting it go completely, knowing your true nature is always clean.

verses.philosophy: This is the state of *Kaivalya* (absolute autonomy). The illusion of personal credit and debit dissolves when you realize you are the spacious witness, completely independent of the mind-body container's past actions.

verses.sanskrit:
ब्रह्मादिस्तम्बपर्यन्तं अहमेवेति निश्चयी ।
निर्विकल्पः शुचिः शान्तः प्राप्ताप्राप्ते न चिन्तयेत् ॥
verses.transliteration:
brahmādistambaparyantaṃ ahamēvēti niścayī |
nirvikalpaḥ śuciḥ śāntaḥ prāptāprāptē na cintayēt ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain, 'From the highest cosmic creator down to a single blade of grass, everything is an expression of myself,' becomes undivided, pure, and quiet. They stop worrying about what they have or lack.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra expands identity to the absolute maximum. If you are the very fabric out of which every form is woven, the concept of missing out on something or needing to compete with someone disappears. The mind stops comparing and settles into deep, universal peace.

understand.common_misconception: Feeling small, isolated, and continuously jealous of other people's assets, success, or life paths.
understand.attainment_meaning: Recognizing that everyone's life is just a different expression of the same shared consciousness, which allows you to celebrate their success as your own.

verses.philosophy: This is *Sarvatma-Bhava*—the realization of absolute non-dual unity. When there is no second entity left outside of you, the mental habit of chasing (*prapta*) or avoiding (*aprapta*) naturally runs out of fuel.

verses.sanskrit:
आश्चर्यमिदं विश्वं नास्ति किञ्चिदिति निश्चयी ।
निर्वासनो स्फूर्तिमात्रो न किञ्चिदिव शाम्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
āṣcaryamidaṃ viśvaṃ nāsti kiñciditi niścayī |
nirvāsanō sphūrtimātrō na kiñcidiva śāmyati ||
verses.english: One who knows for certain that this diverse universe is a marvelous illusion with no independent reality, becomes entirely desireless. Remaining as pure, radiant presence, they dissolve into absolute peace.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra closes the chapter by describing the natural rest of an integrated soul. The universe is viewed as a fascinating, temporary show (*āṣcaryam*). Because the sage no longer expects the show to be permanent or separate from them, their latent cravings (*vasanas*) dissolve completely, allowing them to rest as pure, radiant presence.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting that discovering non-duality will turn you into a cold, clinical, and completely bored person who hates looking at life.
understand.attainment_meaning: It allows you to look at the world with lighthearted wonder and appreciation, entirely free from the heavy burden of anxiety.

verses.philosophy: This lands on *Nirvasana Sthiti*—the desireless state. The universe is recognized as an insubstantial appearance (*Mithya*), allowing awareness to stop seeking external fulfillment and rest completely in its own continuous, peaceful nature.

title: Sadhana Vishranti
description: The Absolute Cessation of Spiritual Effort
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Sadhana Vishranti Prakaranam (The Cessation of Spiritual Effort), King Janaka speaks once again. Responding to Ashtavakra’s deep teachings on wisdom and divine order, Janaka describes his actual internal experience of dropping all methods, techniques, and practices. He reveals the ultimate paradox of Advaita: that forcing your mind to meditate or sit in rigid postures actually keeps the separate, anxious ego alive. True realization is the complete relaxation of all effort, resting effortlessly in your natural state.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
कायकृत्यात्सहस्तस्य ततो वाक्कमंतोऽभवम् ।
चिन्तयास्मादतोऽभवं एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
kāyakṛtyātsahastasya tatō vākkamantō'bhavam |
cintayāsmādātō'bhavaṃ ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Janaka said: First I became weary of physical rituals and actions; then I became weary of lengthy speech and philosophical discussions; finally, I became weary of mental concentration and thinking. Therefore, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka walks us through his process of stepping down from effort. He realizes that mechanical physical movements, exhausting intellectual debates, and even the heavy task of forcing his thoughts to remain silent are all driven by the anxious ego trying to change reality. He drops the management and simply relaxes.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that staying enlightened requires you to spend your whole life executing complex physical postures, chanting formulas, or constantly monitoring your thoughts.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that your true nature is the wide-open, effortless space that remains when all spiritual exercises and chores are completely finished.

verses.philosophy: This is the transition from *Sadhana* (active practices) to *Sadhya* (the final goal). The Self is not a produced result of any action (*Kriya*); it is the ever-present ground that is revealed when the ego stops striving.

verses.sanskrit:
प्रीत्यभावेन शब्दादेरदृश्यत्वेन चात्मनः ।
विक्षेपैकाग्र्यहृदय एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
prītyabhāvēna śabdādēradṛśyatvēna cātmanaḥ |
vikṣēpaikāgryahṛdaya ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Because I no longer depend on sensory sounds and objects for my happiness, and because the true Self cannot be seen as an external object, my mind is free from both distraction and forced concentration. Therefore, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: The mind usually alternates between two stressful states: being distracted by sensory objects (*vikshepa*) or forcing itself into tight concentration (*ekagrya*) to block the world out. Janaka steps out of this battle. Since he doesn't need objects to feel whole, his mind naturally settles into peace without any force.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that a peaceful mind is a dead, frozen zone where thoughts are blocked from ever appearing.
understand.attainment_meaning: Allowing thoughts to come and go naturally like passing clouds, without chasing them or fighting them.

verses.philosophy: Forced concentration implies a split between the meditator and the object of meditation. When the false value assigned to worldly objects drops, the mind enters a state of *Nirvikalpa*—natural, strainless resting.

verses.sanskrit:
अध्यासादिभ्रमाद्यस्मादेवं पश्यामि संस्थितम् ।
गतक्लेशो विमुक्तात्मा एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
adhyāsādibhramādyasmādēvaṃ paśyāmi saṃsthitam |
gataklēśō vimuktātmā ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Recognizing that mental distortions like false superimposition are just a trick of perspective, I view things exactly as they are. Free from distress and anchored in freedom, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka looks at how the mind layers stories onto reality. A situation happens, and the mind overlays a huge narrative of anxiety onto it. By separating the raw present moment from the mind's dramatic commentary, the imaginary weight of his problems vanishes instantly.

understand.common_misconception: Blaming your daily events, your history, or other people for making you feel emotionally exhausted and trapped.
understand.attainment_meaning: Seeing that your stress is created entirely by the stories your mind tells you about events, not by the events themselves.

verses.philosophy: This is the dissolving of *Adhyasa* (false cognitive overlay). When the mind stops creating artificial interpretations, the world is seen clearly as an innocent display, leaving the observer unbothered.

verses.sanskrit:
बन्धमोक्षौ न मे चिन्ता न च शान्तिः कल्पिता ।
ज्ञानान्मन्मात्रनिष्ठानामेवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
bandhamōkṣau na mē cintā na ca śāntiḥ kalpitā |
jñānānmānmātraniṣṭhānāmēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Worrying about being bound or being free is no longer a concern for me, nor do I need to manufacture an artificial state of peace. Recognizing that everything is an expression of pure awareness, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka highlights a massive spiritual trap: obsessing over whether you are acting 'enlightened' enough or trying to force yourself to feel peaceful. True peace is not a fragile mood that you have to protect; it is your baseline nature.

understand.common_misconception: Treating spiritual awakening as an ongoing, stressful acting job where you must always look, speak, and feel perfectly calm.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the spiritual performance entirely and resting as the natural presence that hosts whatever mood is present.

verses.philosophy: Worrying about liberation (*Moksha-Chinta*) is still a movement of the separate ego. Shifting identity completely to pure consciousness (*Chinmatra*) reveals that your identity was never in a state of conflict.

verses.sanskrit:
कृतमकृतं च कर्मास्ति क्व च मे भावना अधुना ।
एवमप्युत्थितं सम्यगेवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
kṛtamakṛtaṃ ca karmāsti kva ca mē bhāvanā adhunā |
ēvamapyutthitaṃ samyagēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: What meaning is left in duties performed or duties neglected for me? Free from all calculation, I let life's actions unfold spontaneously through me, remaining settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka drops the mental scorecard of 'what I should have done' or 'what I need to do next.' He stops calculating his life like a business transaction. He allows his body and mind to act naturally to fulfill his royal duties, while his internal awareness remains perfectly still.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that dropping your mental calculations will turn you into an irresponsible, lazy person who can no longer get anything done.
understand.attainment_meaning: Your actions actually become cleaner and more efficient because they are driven by the needs of the present moment rather than egoic fear.

verses.philosophy: This is the transcendence of *Kartritva-Abhimana* (the sense of personal agency). When actions are seen as natural movements of the material universe (*Prakriti*), the witness remains completely clean.

verses.sanskrit:
अक्रियत्वाच्छरीरस्य क्व मे कर्म क्व भावना ।
आत्मा निष्क्रिय एवायं एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
akriyatvāccharīrasya kva mē karma kva bhāvanā |
ātmā niṣkriya ēvāyaṃ ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: Since the body performs actions on its own relative plane, and the true Self is inherently actionless, what meaning is left in concepts of karma or duty? Realizing this, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka observes a clear division of labor. The physical body walks, breathes, eats, and speaks according to its design—just like a tree grows or water flows. The true Self doesn't participate in these physical movements; it is the wide-open space that observes them.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming your consciousness must feel heavy and exhausted after a long day of physical work or running errands.
understand.attainment_meaning: Your body might feel tired, but your internal awareness remains completely spacious, fresh, and untouched by the work.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Nishkriya* (inherently actionless). All behavioral movements are properties of the material mechanism. Realizing this absolute boundary prevents any psychological strain or exhaustion.

verses.sanskrit:
न मे लाभो न मे हानिः क्व मे सङ्गो न वा गतिः ।
निरपेक्षोऽहं शुद्धोऽहं एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
na mē lābhō na mē hāniḥ kva mē saṅgō na vā gatiḥ |
nirapēkṣō'haṃ śuddhō'haṃ ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: I experience neither gain nor loss. Where is any attachment, movement, or path for me? Independent and pure, I am now settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka closes his declaration of effortless rest by pointing out that a path is only necessary for someone who needs to travel to a new location. Since he is already the infinite space of awareness, there is nowhere left for him to travel, nothing left to win, and nothing left to lose.

understand.common_misconception: Viewing the spiritual path as an endless, competitive ladder where you must continuously chase the next level of achievement.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that you are already at the destination, which allows you to stop traveling and finally enjoy the present moment.

verses.philosophy: This is *Nirapeksha*—absolute independence. When awareness realizes it is the continuous context of all life, it drops the illusion of linear progress and rests beautifully in its own natural presence.

verses.sanskrit:
एवं विमृशतो नित्यं निर्विकल्पे स्थितिर्मम ।
स्वेच्छाप्रवृत्तेः कर्मापि कुर्वतोऽप्यन्यवत्सुखम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
ēvaṃ vimṛśatō nityaṃ nirvikalpē sthitirmama |
svēcchāpravṛttēḥ karmāpi kurvatō'pyanyavatsukham ||
verses.english: By continuously resting in this unconditioned state, my mind is completely quiet. Even when performing spontaneous actions according to the needs of the moment, I remain happy and unattached, like a casual observer.
verses.commentary: Janaka concludes Chapter 12 by describing his integrated life. He doesn't sit like a rigid stone statue to stay free. He rules his kingdom, talks to his people, and handles challenges. Because he does it all without the desperate ego claiming ownership, he moves through life with total, lighthearted joy.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting that deep realization will make you an awkward, detached person who can no longer connect with everyday human activities.
understand.attainment_meaning: It allows you to engage with the everyday world with deep presence and warmth, precisely because you aren't trying to exploit situations to feel safe.

verses.philosophy: This is *Sahaja Samadhi*—the natural, integrated absorption. The sage is completely *Nirvikalpa* (free of conceptual projection) on the inside, while allowing his body to perform actions (*Karma*) smoothly on the outside, living as a perfectly free, living expression of absolute reality.

title: Laya Sthiti
description: The State of Continuous Inner Quiet
text: In this chapter, traditionally titled Laya Sthiti Prakaranam (The State of Continuous Inner Quiet), King Janaka describes the natural behavior of an awakened being. He explains how his mind operates now that his ego has dissolved. A sage does not force themselves to follow rigid lifestyle rules; instead, they move through the world with the innocent spontaneity of a child, acting when action is needed, resting when rest is needed, and remaining completely unattached to any identity.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
अकिंचनभवं स्वास्थ्यं कौपीनत्वेऽपि दुर्लभम् ।
त्यागादाने विहायास्मादहमासे यथासुखम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
akiñcanabhavaṃ svāsthyaṃ kaupīnatvē'pi durlabham |
tyāgādānē vihāyāsmādahamāsē yathāsukham ||
verses.english: Janaka said: That pristine inner health which comes from owning nothing is rare and hard to find, even for a ascetic wearing only a loincloth. Therefore, dropping all ideas of giving up or holding onto things, I live happily.
verses.commentary: Janaka exposes the trap of superficial spirituality. Many people think that giving up their money, wearing simple rags, or running away to a forest will automatically quiet their minds. Janaka notes that true detachment is an inside job: if a monk is secretly proud of how little they own, their ego is still running the show. Janaka chooses to drop the mental categories of 'giving up' versus 'keeping' entirely.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must change your wardrobe, drop your career, or live in absolute poverty to find genuine peace and presence.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that freedom is breaking your psychological dependence on things to define your value, allowing you to live comfortably exactly where you are.

verses.philosophy: True *Akiñchana* (owning nothing) is a state of awareness, not a lack of physical items. When the mind stops creating the illusion of ownership, you enter a state of total autonomy (*Svasthyam*).

verses.sanskrit:
कुत्रापि खेदः कायस्य जिव्हा कुत्रापि खिद्यते ।
मनः कुत्रापि तत्तयक्त्वा पुरुषार्थे स्थितोऽस्म्यहम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
kutrāpi khēdaḥ kāyasya jivhā kutrāpi khidyatē |
manaḥ kutrāpi tattayaktvā puruṣārthē sthitō'smyaham ||
verses.english: Sometimes the body experiences fatigue, sometimes the tongue grows tired of speaking, and sometimes the mind feels weary of thinking. Dropping all of them, I rest securely in the ultimate goal of human life.
verses.commentary: Janaka observes the natural limitations of his human vehicle. The physical body gets tired after a busy day, the voice gets fatigued after speaking, and the mind gets drained after long analytical tasks. Janaka stops panicking about these natural changes. Instead of trying to fix a tired mind or body, he steps back and rests as the silent witness of the fatigue.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting spiritual awakening to transform your physical body and mind into an invincible, superhuman machine that never experiences exhaustion or low energy.
understand.attainment_meaning: Allowing your body and mind to have natural cycles of energy and fatigue without letting those shifting states disrupt your inner peace.

verses.philosophy: The witness is *Nirvifara* (changeless). Sickness, health, energy, and fatigue are properties of the material vehicle (*Prakriti*). Realizing this prevents you from taking physical states personally.

verses.sanskrit:
कृतं किमपि नैव स्यादिति संचिन्त्य तत्त्वतः ।
यदा यत्कर्तुमायाति तत्कृत्वास्यासे यथासुखम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
kṛtaṃ kimapi naiva syāditi sañcintya tattvataḥ |
yadā yatkartumāyāti tatkṛtvāsyāsē yathāsukham ||
verses.english: Recognizing that from the absolute standpoint nothing ever truly happens, I handle whatever duty naturally presents itself in the moment and live happily.
verses.commentary: Janaka adopts an incredibly practical yet deep approach to daily life. He knows that in the grand scheme of time, his current problems and actions are minor ripples. This broad perspective removes the stress of perfectionism, allowing him to handle his immediate work fluidly and calmly.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking that realizing 'nothing matters in the long run' will turn you into a cold, careless person who slacks off and ignores commitments.
understand.attainment_meaning: It removes the paralyzing anxiety of overthinking, making you highly effective because you meet situations without an agenda.

verses.philosophy: This lands on the state of *Akarta* (non-doer). Actions happen spontaneously according to the needs of the environment (*Prarabdha*), while your awareness remains completely clear.

verses.sanskrit:
कर्मबद्धाः शरीराढ्या निष्कर्मत्वं न कुत्रचित् ।
वाङ्मनस्यां विहायास्मादहमासे यथासुखम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
karmabaddhāḥ śarīrāḍhyā niṣkarmatvaṃ na kutracit |
vāṅmanasyāṃ vihaāyāsmādahamāsē yathāsukham ||
verses.english: For a person who identifies deeply with the body, absolute physical inaction is impossible. Therefore, dropping the mental anxiety of trying to force my mind and voice to be silent, I live happily.
verses.commentary: Janaka exposes another common mistake: trying to achieve spiritual peace by sitting completely still and aggressively forcing your thoughts to shut up. As long as you have a body, it will have basic functions—breathing, digestions, and brain activity. Janaka drops the heavy task of trying to manage his thoughts, letting his mind tick naturally while he rests as the observer.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you are failing spiritually if your mind produces a thought or a memory while you are trying to relax.
understand.attainment_meaning: Letting thoughts float through your mind like background noise on a city street, without tuning in or fighting them.

verses.philosophy: Forcing the mind to be still (*Chitta-Nirodha*) through sheer willpower keeps the separate ego alive. True freedom is letting the mind do its job naturally while you stay out of the way.

verses.sanskrit:
विहिताविहितं नास्ति न च चिन्ता ममास्ति वै ।
संन्यस्य संन्यासं एवमेवाहमास्थितः ॥
verses.transliteration:
vihitāvihitaṃ nāsti na ca cintā mamāsti vai |
saṃnyasya saṃnyāsaṃ ēvamēvāhamāsthitaḥ ||
verses.english: For me, there are no more absolute rules of 'what is mandatory' or 'what is forbidden,' nor do I worry about them. Having given up even the concept of renunciation itself, I am settled exactly as I am.
verses.commentary: Janaka steps beyond conventional spiritual logic. He has dropped all fear of religious or social rules. He has even dropped the identity of being a 'renunciant' or an 'enlightened person.' Why? Because wearing a spiritual label implies that you are still playing an egoic role.

understand.common_misconception: Treating spirituality like a competitive job where you must constantly perform and act out a specific, holy lifestyle to look perfect.
understand.attainment_meaning: Dropping the spiritual performance entirely, allowing yourself to be simple, open, and authentic.

verses.philosophy: This is *Sannyasa-Sannyasa*—the renunciation of the concept of renunciation. When your identity merges completely with reality, spiritual methods and definitions are recognized as temporary scaffolds that are no longer needed.

verses.sanskrit:
अचेष्टमपि संचिन्त्य चेष्टमानं शरीरतः ।
अधुना कुत्रचित् कश्चिद् आत्मा आलोक्यते मया ॥
verses.transliteration:
acēṣṭamapi sañcintya cēṣṭamānaṃ śarīrataḥ |
adhunā kutracit kaścid ātmā ālokyate mayā ||
verses.english: Though my physical body continues to execute actions, I remain completely actionless inside. At all times and in all conditions, I perceive only my true nature.
verses.commentary: Janaka experiences a complete shift in perspective. To an outside observer, King Janaka looks like he is busy managing his kingdom—signing decrees, talking to advisors, or walking through his palace. But inside, Janaka feels like a quiet observer sitting comfortably in a dark cinema, watching his body move across life's screen.

understand.common_misconception: Assuming that finding deep inner quiet means you will freeze like a statue and stop interacting with your family or job.
understand.attainment_meaning: You can be highly active and engage with life fully on the outside, while remaining completely silent and still on the inside.

verses.philosophy: This is the state of *Karma-Akarma*—action in inaction. The body fulfills its worldly responsibilities naturally, while awareness remains completely anchored in its own still nature.

verses.sanskrit:
एवं विमृशतो नित्यं निर्विकल्पे स्थितिर्मम ।
स्वेच्छाप्रवृत्तेः कर्मापि कुर्वतोऽप्यन्यवत्सुखम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
ēvaṃ vimṛśatō nityaṃ nirvikalpē sthitirmama |
svēcchāpravṛttēḥ karmāpi kurvatō'pyanyavatsukham ||
verses.english: Living naturally in this unconditioned state, I remain completely quiet. Even when performing spontaneous duties, I move through life with total ease, happy and unattached like a casual observer.
verses.commentary: Janaka closes the chapter by describing his integrated lifestyle. He doesn't need to protect his peace or isolate himself to stay clear. He rules his kingdom, handles daily challenges, and talks to people. Because he acts without an anxious ego demanding validation, his life becomes an effortless, beautiful dance.

understand.common_misconception: Fearing that spiritual awakening will turn you into an awkward, cold person who can no longer enjoy normal human activities.
understand.attainment_meaning: It allows you to enjoy and experience the world with deep warmth and presence, precisely because you aren't trying to exploit situations to feel safe.

verses.philosophy: This lands on *Sahaja Samadhi*—natural absorption. The sage is *Nirvikalpa* (free of conceptual projection) on the inside, while allowing actions to flow smoothly through their body on the outside, living as a free, living expression of absolute reality.

title: Ishvara Prakaranam
description: The Natural and Spontaneous Quietude of the Mind
text: In this brief but incredibly deep chapter, traditionally titled Ishvara Prakaranam (The Plane of Natural Dissolution of the Mind), King Janaka explains the inner psychological reality of an awakened mind. He describes a state where thoughts and desires no longer leave any sticky residue or emotional baggage. Instead of forcefully silencing his mind, his thoughts have naturally lost their driving power, allowing his awareness to remain clear, open, and at peace.

verses.sanskrit:
जनक उवाच -
प्रकृत्या शून्यचित्तो यः प्रमादाद्भावभावनः ।
निद्रितो बोधित इव क्षीणसंसरणो हि सः ॥
verses.transliteration:
janaka uvāca -
prakṛtyā śūnyacitto yaḥ pramādādbhāvabhāvanḥ |
nidrito bodhita iva kṣīṇasaṃsaraṇo hi saḥ ||
verses.english: Janaka said: One whose mind is naturally empty of conceptual projections, and who only thinks of external things by chance or casual necessity, is like a person awake while appearing to sleep. For them, the cycle of wandering is over.
verses.commentary: Janaka describes the default baseline of an awakened mind: it is naturally empty (*śūnya*) of repetitive, obsessive self-talk. Thoughts only arise when a practical situation demands them, and once the task is finished, the mind snaps back to clear space. To an outside observer, they might look passive or asleep, but inside, they are wide awake.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that an enlightened person must spend every second manually monitoring their brain to suppress thoughts from appearing.
understand.attainment_meaning: Thoughts naturally settle down on their own when you stop fueling them with anxious self-importance, leaving your mind clean and quiet.

verses.philosophy: This is the state of *Sahaja Amanibhava*—natural mindlessness. The mind loses its habitual, frantic momentum (*Samsara*) because your identification has shifted entirely to the silent witness.

verses.sanskrit:
क्व धनानि क्व मित्राणि क्व मे विषयदस्यवः ।
क्व शास्त्रं क्व च विज्ञानं यदा मे गळिता स्पृहा ॥
verses.transliteration:
kva dhanāni kva mitrāṇi kva mē viṣayadasyavaḥ |
kva śāstraṃ kva ca vijñānaṃ yadā mē gāḷitā spṛhā ||
verses.english: Where is wealth, where are friends, where are the thieves of sense objects, where are scriptures, and where is intellectual knowledge, now that my cravings have completely dissolved?
verses.commentary: Janaka lists things that usually dominate human life: wealth, social circles, sensory temptations (which he calls 'thieves' because they steal presence), and even intellectual or scriptural knowledge. When your deep psychological craving (*spṛhā*) dissolves, these items lose their power over you. They might still exist around you physically, but they can no longer define your worth.

understand.common_misconception: Thinking you must physically throw away your bank accounts, delete your contacts, or burn your books to be truly detached.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that you can have wealth, friends, and books, but you no longer use them as emotional crutches to feel complete.

verses.philosophy: When *Trishna* (craving) is removed, objects lose their superficial value layout (*Adhyasa*). The entire dualistic framework of seeking outside assets drops away because you are anchored in your own fullness.

verses.sanskrit:
विज्ञाते साक्षिपुरुषे परमात्मनि चेश्वरे ।
निराकाङ्क्षे च बन्धे च मोक्षे च न मम स्थितिः ॥
verses.transliteration:
vijñātē sākṣipuruṣē paramātmani cēśvarē |
nirākāṅkṣē ca bandhē ca mōkṣē ca na mama sthitiḥ ||
verses.english: Having realized my true nature as the absolute witness, the supreme self, and the core of cosmic order, I am entirely desireless. I no longer inhabit the concepts of bondage or liberation.
verses.commentary: Janaka steps completely out of the spiritual game. As long as you are tracking yourself as a 'seeker' trying to get 'liberated,' you are still validating the false story that you are bound. By recognizing that he is already the spacious witness hosting the whole cosmic play, the concepts of being trapped or being freed lose all meaning.

understand.common_misconception: Treating liberation like a permanent retirement badge or high status that your spiritual ego can carry around proudly.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is realizing that the separate entity who was stressing over being bound or free was an illusion all along.

verses.philosophy: This is the transition beyond *Sadhana-Duality*. Bondage (*Bandha*) and Liberation (*Moksha*) are relative concepts that exist only within the mind. The absolute witness (*Sakshi*) stands completely independent of both categories.

verses.sanskrit:
अन्तर्विकल्पशून्यस्य बहिः स्वच्छन्दचारिणः ।
भ्रान्तस्येव दशास्तास्तास्तादृशा एव जानते ॥
verses.transliteration:
antarvikalpaśūnyasya bahiḥ svacchandacāriṇaḥ |
bhrāntasyēva daśāstāstāstādṛśā ēva jānatē ||
verses.english: Completely empty of inner conceptual conflict, yet moving spontaneously in the external world according to its own laws, the unique state of such an individual can only be understood by those who share it.
verses.commentary: Janaka concludes the chapter by describing how a sage looks from the outside. To an ordinary observer, an enlightened being might look unpredictable, eccentric, or like a drifter (*bhrāntasyēva*). They don't fit into standard societal slots because they don't act from calculation, worry, or social pressure. They act out of pure, spontaneous presence, a reality that can only be truly recognized by another free soul.

understand.common_misconception: Expecting an enlightened person to act like a highly predictable, standardized saint who follows a rigid, mechanical script at all times.
understand.attainment_meaning: They are simple, authentic, and completely fluid, matching their actions perfectly to the raw truth of the current moment.

verses.philosophy: This is the unconditioned behavior of a *Jivanmukta*. Internally they are *Vikalpa-Sunya* (free of mental conflict), while externally they practice *Svachandachari* (spontaneous, unrestricted movement), flowing naturally with life as a living expression of absolute consciousness.

title: Yathatathyam
description: The Exposition of Absolute Reality
text: In this landmark chapter, traditionally titled Yathatathyam Prakaranam (The Exposition of Absolute Reality), the sage Ashtavakra speaks again. He delivers a profound stream of direct non-dual insights, systematically guiding King Janaka to stabilize his awakening. Ashtavakra strips away all remaining dependencies on intellectual studying, moral scorecards, states of meditation, and physical definitions, leaving awareness to rest seamlessly in its own luminous, unconditioned reality.

verses.sanskrit:
अष्टावक्र उवाच -
यथातथोपदेशेन कृतार्थः सत्त्वबुद्धिमान् ।
आजीवमपि जिज्ञासुः परस्तत्र विमुह्यति ॥
verses.transliteration:
aṣṭāvakra uvāca -
yathātathōpadēśēna kṛtārthaḥ sattvabuddhimān |
ājīvamapi jijñāsuḥ parastatra vimuhyati ||
verses.english: Ashtavakra said: A person of pure intellect achieves fulfillment simply by hearing this direct, unvarnished truth. An insincere seeker, however, remains confused and lost even if they spend their entire life analyzing it.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra highlights that readiness is everything. If the mind is calm and ready, the truth drops in and works instantly like an explosion of clarity. But if the mind is still caught up in intellectual pride, it treats the truth as a complex puzzle, overthinking it instead of living it.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that you must accumulate decades of complex training and memorize vast books to earn inner freedom.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that freedom is immediate and simple; it requires a quiet, sincere mind that is ready to stop playing games.

verses.philosophy: This addresses *Sattva-Buddhi*—the purified intellect. When the mind's distracting habits are quieted, the non-dual truth is recognized instantly via *Shravana-Matra* (the mere hearing of it).

verses.sanskrit:
मोक्षो विषयवैरस्यं बन्धो विषयरक्तता ।
एतावदेव विज्ञानं यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु ॥
verses.transliteration:
mōkṣō viṣayavairasyaṃ bandhō viṣayaraktatā |
etāvadēva vijñānaṃ yathēcchasi tathā kuru ||
verses.english: Liberation is simply the cooling down of interest in sensory objects; bondage is the continuous, desperate hunger for them. This is the ultimate core of wisdom. Now, do exactly as you wish.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra distills the entire landscape of spiritual theory down to a single binary. You don't need to overcomplicate things with grand theological frameworks. If your mind is constantly grasping for external patches, it is in bondage. If your mind is content in itself, it is free. Ashtavakra throws the choice completely back to Janaka.

understand.common_misconception: Treating liberation as an elite supernatural reward or location that you unlock through complex spiritual trades.
understand.attainment_meaning: It is simply the psychological state where external conditions can no longer compromise your internal wholeness.

verses.philosophy: This is the absolute compression of *Vairagya* (dispassion) and *Raga* (attachment). Awareness doesn't need to manipulate the external world; it simply needs to see through its power to bind.

verses.sanskrit:
इदं भव्यक्षरोद्भासि वाक्यं सत्यमयं शृणु ।
न त्वं देहो न ते देहो भोक्ता कर्ता न वा भवान् ॥
verses.transliteration:
idaṃ bhavyaṣarōdbhāsi vākyaṃ satyamayaṃ śṛṇu |
na tvaṃ dēhō na tē dēhō bhōktā kartā na vā bhavān ||
verses.english: Listen closely to this supreme, immutable truth: You are not the physical body, nor does the body possess you. You are neither the active doer nor the emotional experiencer.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra hits the primary baseline of identification. The body is a biological mechanism that ages and changes. The ego is a mental mechanism that alternates between feeling proud of its actions (*doer*) or victimized by events (*experiencer*). You are the spacious awareness that watches both mechanisms click along.

understand.common_misconception: Feeling deep personal guilt, physical shame, and heavy anxiety over your body's history or actions.
understand.attainment_meaning: Caring for your body naturally while recognizing that your baseline consciousness remains pristine and independent of its changes.

verses.philosophy: The Self is fundamentally *Akarta* (non-doer) and *Abhokta* (non-experiencer). Shifting into this stance cancels out the psychological generator of karma.

verses.sanskrit:
त्वं तु सर्वगतः पूर्णः शिवः साक्ष्यक्रियात्मकः ।
अनावृतो निराकाङ्क्षः शान्तस्त्वं सुखं चर ॥
verses.transliteration:
tvaṃ tu sarvadataḥ pūrṇaḥ śivaḥ sākṣyakriyātmakaḥ |
anāvṛtō nirākāṅkṣaḥ śāntastvaṃ sukhaṃ cara ||
verses.english: You are all-pervading, complete, auspicious, the actionless witness, limitless, desireless, and quiet. Live happily and move through life with ease.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra lists the true attributes of Janaka's presence. Awareness is not an isolated bubble locked up inside a skull; it is the wide-open fabric in which the perception of skulls, rooms, and stars takes place. It is already whole (*pūrṇaḥ*), needing no additions to be secure.

understand.common_misconception: Viewing yourself as a tiny, fractured piece of life that must constantly hustle to fix its broken energy.
understand.attainment_meaning: Stepping back into the boundless space of your presence, recognizing you are already safe and whole.

verses.philosophy: The nature of consciousness is *Sva-Mahima*—inherently majestic. Because it is *Anavrita* (unveiled/limitless), it contains all change without absorbing any damage.

verses.sanskrit:
त्वं सङ्गमुक्तो विमलो धीरस्त्यक्तक्रियात्मकः ।
एतदेव हि ते बन्धः समाधिमनुतिष्ठसि ॥
verses.transliteration:
tvaṃ saṅgamuktō vimalō dhīrastyaktakriyātmakaḥ |
etadēva hi te bandhaḥ samādhimanutiṣṭhasi ||
verses.english: You are unattached, stainless, steady, and naturally still. Your true bondage is that you keep practicing formal meditation techniques to capture this.
verses.commentary: Ashtavakra delivers a shocking, radical warning. Forcing your mind to achieve extreme concentration states (*samadhi*) is an act of subtle aggression. It implies you think you are currently broken and need to manufacture peace. True peace is your natural state; trying to practice your way into it keeps the separate seeker alive.

understand.common_misconception: Believing that continuous spiritual exercises, forced breathing tricks, and mental trances are the absolute goal of freedom.
understand.attainment_meaning: Realizing that trying to quiet your thoughts implies you think you are the thoughts, when you are actually the space observing them.

verses.philosophy: The Self is *Sva-Siddha*—already accomplished. Relying on conditional meditation states makes your freedom dependent on practices, which is the definition of bondage.

verses.sanskrit:
त्वया व्याप्तमिदं विश्वं त्वयि प्रोतं यथार्थतः ।
शुद्धबुद्धस्वरूपस्त्वं मा गमः क्षुद्रचित्तताम् ॥
verses.transliteration:
tvayā vyāptamidaṃ viśvaṃ tvayi prōtaṃ yathārhataḥ |
śuddhabuddhasvarūpastvaṃ mā gamaḥ kṣudracittatām ||
